questions.retrievalTag: Adolescents are commonly dissatisfied with their weight and body shape
questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She feels well but says she worries about her weight; she reports that for as long as she can remember, she has been taller and larger than the other kids in her class. She is a cheerleader at her middle school and describes herself as the heaviest girl on the squad. Her attempts to lose weight through diet have been unsuccessful. She usually eats three meals daily and tries to eat a lot of vegetables and fruit. She also exercises daily, often running or bicycling on days she does not have cheerleading practice. She reports that she is a good student and has many friends. Menarche was at the age of 11 years. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She appears healthy and somewhat older than her stated age. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 52 kg (115 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Breast and genital development are sexual maturity rating stage 4. She has a full range of affect and normal thought process. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adolescents are commonly dissatisfied with their weight and body shape, often fueled by an unrealistically thin beauty ideal. Dieting and a degree of concern about body weight are considered normal in adolescents.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: TEES TL This high-functioning patient demonstrates normal adolescent eating behavior and normal exercise habits for an athlete. Adolescents are commonly dissatisfied with their weight and body shape. This dissatisfaction is likely a function of education about obesity and nutrition as well as media images of an unrealistically thin beauty ideal, particularly for girls and women. Sports that involve body aesthetics such as cheerleading and dance may be more likely to lead to body image concerns. AS such, dieting is popular among adolescents. The physician should normalize the patient's concer about body weight and reassure the patient that her body weight is healthy.

questions.retrievalTag: Schizophrenia is characterized by at least six months exhibiting two
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the office by his parents because of increasingly erratic behavior. He completed college without difficulty but moved back to his parents' home 5 years ago because he was having financial difficulties. He is an aspiring writer and has begun writing several novels and travel guides. One year ago, he began to collect junk mail and papers in his bedroom and would not allow his parents to discard any trash. He began to wear the same clothes for several days in a row. During the past month, he has refused to leave his bedroom and now spends all his time working on his computer. He says that he is doing internet research for his writing projects and sending e-mails to his friends. Last week, his parents discovered that he had not been connected to the internet for 3 months, and his e-mail account showed no activity for the past 4 months. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not drink alcoholic beverages or use illicit drugs. He is poorly groomed and slightly overweight. Physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, most of his replies are "yes" or "no." He describes his mood as "okay" and has a flat affect. He says he is worried only about his computer work and requests that his parents not delete anything because his writing is "important" and "not ready." There is no evidence of hallucinations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Schizophrenia is characterized by at least six months exhibiting two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia, poor grooming).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). This patient demonstrates prominent negative symptoms (eg, poor grooming as evidence of apathy) along with disorganized thinking/behavior (stating he is sending e-mails despite not connecting to internet) and evidence of delusional thinking {eg, not allowing his parents to discard trash for an unclear reason). Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and social withdrawal. To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed six months. Treatment requires antipsychotic, medications.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Delusional disorder (Choice A) presents with fixed delusions in the absence of other psychiatric symptoms such as hallucinations, disorganization, depression, or 'mania. This patient's negative symptoms are more consistent with schizophrenia, Intellectual developmental disorder (Choice B) refers to a neurodevelopmental disorder beginning in childhood, featuring intellectual challenges and challenges in social and practical functioning. This patient instead demonstrates a slow deciine in functioning beginning in adulthood. Additionally, this patient's negative symptoms are more consistent with schizophrenia, 'Obsessive-compulsive disorder (Choice C) manifests with recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts (obsessions) that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals (compulsions). Rather than repeating actions, this patient's activity level has decreased, and he appears apathetic rather than anxious, consistent with the negative symptoms of schizophrenia, Paranoid personality disorder (Choice D) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster, characterized by a pervasive mistrust of others and viewing others as possessing malicious intent. This patient's degree of negative symptoms is more consistent with schizophrenia. Also, there is no clear evidence that the patient views others as possessing malicious intent.

questions.retrievalTag: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome commonly occurs within a few days of
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-day history of headache, anxiety, nausea, muscle aches, and diarrhea. Ibuprofen has not fully resolved the headache. She was diagnosed with major depressive disorder 8 months ago, but she has discontinued the dosage of her sertraline during the past month because she is trying to conceive. She has not had any alcoholic beverages during the past 2 weeks; she previously consumed six glasses of wine weekly. She does not use any illicit drugs. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a mild tremor in both hands. On mental status examination, she describes her mood as "worried" and has a tired affect. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome commonly occurs within a few days of abrupt discontinuation of chronic antidepressant medication. Patients present with flu-like symptoms, gastrointestinal upset, dizziness, sensory disturbances, and hyperarousal symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: 'Approximately 20% of patients develop antidepressant discontinuation syndrome with abrupt discontinuation of chronic antidepressant medication. The syndrome typically involves flu-like symptoms (malaise, diaphoresis, headache, myalgias), gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarthea), vertigo or dizziness, sensory disturbances (paresthesia), and hyperarousal (anxiety, irtability, jerkiness). Symptoms typically begin after a few days of antidepressant discontinuation and resolve within weeks. Risk factors for antidepressant discontinuation syndrome include abrupt discontinuation and short half-life medication. The syndrome can be managed by restarting the antidepressant medication and initiating a slower taper, leading to resolution of symptoms in a few days. Discontinuation symptoms should be distinguished from relapse of anxiety or depressive disorder symptoms, which typically begin and resolve more slowly.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Alcohol use disorder (Choice A) is unlikely, as this patient does not demonstrate alcohol overuse or negative consequences of use. Alcohol withdrawal presents with 'sympathetic activation, but muscle aches and diarthea are uncommon. Influenza (Choice B) presents with fever, cough, sore throat, nasal congestion, myalgias, headaches, and lethargy. Anxiety and tremulousness are less common, and this patient has a different explanation for her flu-like symptoms. Migraine headache (Choice C) is a primary headache associated with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Diarthea and myalgias would be uncommon. Pregnancy (Choice D) can present with nausea, tender breasts, polyuria, fatigue, muscle cramping, and moodiness with somewhat insidious and episodic onset. An acute-onset of flu-ike symptoms (eg, myalgia, headache), anxiety, and diarrhea would be atypical. Unspecified anxiety disorder (Choice F) presents with persistent anxiety symptoms with clinically significant distress or daily dysfunction. This patient is instead experiencing acute symptoms that include flu-like symptoms, which is inconsistent with unspecified anxiety disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within
questions.stem: An 18-year-old man comes to the physician because of constant anxiety for 2 weeks. During this time, he has been unable to stop pacing around his parents' house. He describes his sleep as "okay." He has not had a decreased appetite but does find it difficult to sit still long enough to eat a meal. He is able to go to his summer job but has difficulty concentrating. He says, "I feel like I am crawling out of my skin." His girlfriend left for Europe 3 weeks ago, and he fears that they will grow apart and she will not return. His temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 98/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is agitated and unable to sit still. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within three months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, and/or conduct disturbances. Anxiety symptoms can manifest as worrying, difficulty concentrating, or somatic manifestations such as a restless feeling.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within three months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily school, work, or social functioning. Patients may internalize or externalize their distress, resulting in depression/anxiety symptoms or conduct disturbances (acting out), respectively. Patients with adjustment disorder do not meet criteria of anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. This patient has some anxiety symptoms (eg, worrying, restlessness, difficulty concentrating) with a clear stressor and without 'meeting the criteria for anxiety, depressive, or conduct disorders. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy.

questions.retrievalTag: Mixed features describes when patients simultaneously experience depressive and hypomanic/manic
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician because of insomnia, dysphoria, and irritability for 3 weeks; during this period, she has had a high energy level. She has not been able to focus on her work and has taken numerous sick days. Although she had similar episodes in the past, the episodes have increased in severity and frequency over the past 2 years. She now has up to six episodes yearly. She currently takes no medications. She does not use illicit drugs. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Mixed features describes when patients simultaneously experience depressive and hypomanic/manic symptoms. Any patient who has historically experienced mixed features during mood disorder episodes should be treated with mood stabilizers, rather than antidepressants.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient's presentation is consistent with bipolar disorder with mixed features, involving depressive and manic elements. According to the DSM-S, patients can experience major depressive episodes or hypomanic/manic episodes that feature symptoms from the opposite polarity, which are classified as mixed features (eg, a 'major depressive episode with manic symptoms). Depressive symptoms include depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, psychomotor slowing, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Acute mania typically 'manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Hypomanic episodes feature fewer and less severe manic symptoms compared to acute mania. Major depressive disorder is 'a mood disorder that features major depressive episodes, while bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features hypomanic or manic episodes (with or without 'major depressive episodes). This patient is experiencing an unspecified mood episode that includes both manic (iritability, high energy, decreased need for sleep) and depressive (dysphoria) features. She also demonstrates nonspecific mood symptoms that can occur in either depressive or manic episodes (insomnia, experience major depressive episodes or hypomanic/manic episodes that feature symptoms from the opposite polarity, which are classified as mixed features (eg, a major depressive episode with manic symptoms). Depressive symptoms include depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, psychomotor slowing, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Acute mania typically 'manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Hypomanic episodes feature fewer and less severe manic symptoms compared to acute mania. Major depressive disorder is 'a mood disorder that features major depressive episodes, while bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features hypomanic or manic episodes (with or without 'major depressive episodes). This patient is experiencing an unspecified mood episode that includes both manic (irritability, high energy, decreased need for sleep) and depressive (dysphoria) features. She also demonstrates nonspecific mood symptoms that can occur in either depressive or manic episodes (insomnia, distractibility, work dysfunction). If patients have experienced mood episodes with any hypomanic/manic symptoms, then mood stabilizing medication (eg, lithium, divalproex, atypical antipsychotics) should be utilized. Thus, patients with major depressive disorder with mixed features (a possible diagnosis of this patient), bipolar disorder with mixed features (another possible diagnosis of this patient), or bipolar disorder without mixed features should be treated with mood stabilizing 'medications. Patients with unipolar major depressive disorder without mixed features should be treated with antidepressants.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Alprazolam (Choice A) is a short-acting benzodiazepine that extinguishes anxiety. Its less helpful in mood disorders. 'Amitriptyline (Choice B) is a tricyclic antidepressant utilized in unipolar major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders. It is not commonly used in patients who experience mood episodes with mixed features. Buspirone (Choice C) is a serotonin modulator useful in generalized anxiety disorder. It has no recognized role in mood disorders. Fluoxetine (Choice E) is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor antidepressant utilized in unipolar major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders. It is not 'commonly used in patients who experience mood episodes with mixed features.

questions.retrievalTag: Bad news should be delivered honestly directly and using small
questions.stem: A 57-year-old woman comes to the office to obtain results of evaluation of lower abdominal pain. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis shows a large ovarian mass highly suspicious for ovarian carcinoma as well as numerous small masses throughout the peritoneum. When informed of the findings, the patient expresses disbelief. A few minutes later, she begins to cry. She says that she does not know what she is going to do. Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: "Let's plan on meeting again soon to discuss the situation."

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: "May I step out to get my nurse?"

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: "Please don't be upset. Everything will be ok."

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: "Try to compose yourself or we won't be able to provide you with proper care."

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: "We need to start discussing treatment options now."

questions.educationalObjective: Bad news should be delivered honestly, directly, and using small pieces of information at a time. The physician should create space for patients to express their feelings and then validate their feelings.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bad news (eg, news of a probable or definite life-threatening or life-limiting illness) should be delivered honestly, directly, and using small pieces of information at a time. It is common for patients to cry or express other strong emotions in response to bad news. The physician should create space to discuss the patient's reactions and validate the patient's emotions by listening receptively, using reflective statements, inferring emotions from the expressed content, and normalizing the patient's experience. To discuss the situation, help the patient process her emotions, and continue delivering education in small pieces, itis appropriate for the physician to schedule a follow-up appointment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: "May I step out to get my nurse?" (Choice B) avoids discussion and delegates the conversation to an alternate party. Delegating the conversation about the likely. cancer diagnosis is a common urge physicians may have when delivering difficult news or witnessing strong emotions from a patient. However, itis the physician's, duty to support the patient's emotions while discussing medical information. Delegating further discussion will likely make the patient feel that the physician is dismissing rather than understanding her feelings. "Please don't be upset. Everything will be ok" (Choice C) and "try to compose yourself or we won't be able to provide you with proper care" (Choice D) are responses that ask the patient to compose herself and invalidates or minimizes feelings. Additionally, it is not known whether everything will be ok. These are unempathetic responses that will make the patient feel that the physician is dismissing rather than trying to understand her feelings. The physician should try to understand patients' emotions so that they can provide emotional support and targeted education that will help the patient cope. "We need to start discussing treatment options now' (Choice E) would be premature as the diagnosis is not fully established. In addition, the physician should give the patient time to process the bad news before delivering more information.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of difficulty concentrating during the past 3 months. She says, "I can't think any more, and I can't stop shaking." She also has had difficulty sleeping, decreased level of energy, and a decreased appetite, resulting in a 6.3-kg (14-lb) weight loss. She has stayed home from work for the past 4 days because of her symptoms and worries that she may never be able to work again. She no longer enjoys reading, going out with friends, or watching movies. She has hypertension controlled with labetalol. Her blood pressure is 128/85 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a coarse tremor of the head and upper extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are increased. On mental status examination, she appears dysphoric and tearful. She is ruminative and repeatedly states "I am never going to get better." She wrings her hands and shifts her position in her chair throughout the examination. On cognitive testing, she answers almost every question with "I don't know." Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Symptoms of major depressive disorder include two or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor slowing, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). In some patients, depressive symptoms can be accompanied by anxiety and/or somatic symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by six months or more
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the office to request medication for a 6-year history of anxiety, worry, and insomnia. Three to four nights weekly, he lies in bed, unable to fall asleep and worrying about his life. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He drank six beers daily for 10 years until he was convicted of driving while intoxicated 5 years ago. He now drinks only occasionally and in moderation. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a mildly anxious mood and congruent affect. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation or hallucinations. Results of complete blood count and serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration are within reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by six months or more of excessive worrying about a range of topics and is associated with three additional psychological symptoms (irritability, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, fatigability, sleep disturbance) and/or muscle tension.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive worrying about a range of topics that is associated with three additional psychological symptoms (irritability, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, fatigability, sleep disturbance) and/or muscle tension. Common topics of worry include daily life stressors. The anxiety must occur on more days than not and persist for six months. Treatment should include cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and/or antidepressant 'medication, depending on patient preference and treatment availability. CBT assists patients in changing maladaptive thinking patterns and approaching feared situations,

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Polysomnography (Choice B) is a sleep study performed using specialized equipment to measure oxygenation, end-tidal carbon dioxide, and time spent in various sleep stages. It is used to diagnose sleep apnea, narcolepsy, and other sleep conditions. However, insomnia secondary to generalized anxiety disorder is diagnosed clinically. Propranolol (Choice C) is a beta blocker utilized to treat performance anxiety, tremor, and traumatic brain injury-related agitation. Propranolol is not effective for generalized anxiety disorder. Quetiapine therapy (Choice D) is an atypical antipsychotic medication that primarily antagonizes dopamine-2 receptors and serotonin receptors. Itis a first-line agent for psychotic disorders and can be helpful for refractory generalized anxiety disorder. However, its metabolic adverse effects preclude its use as a first-line generalized anxiety disorder treatment. Recommendation for a 12-step program (Choice E) may ultimately be helpful for this patient's long-term abstinence from alcohol. However, his anxiety is currently 'more impairing than his alcohol use and should be addressed first.

questions.retrievalTag: Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder that involves deficient orexin
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 4-month history of feeling tired in the morning and having difficulty concentrating. He reports feeling sleepy throughout the day and often dozes during important meetings. He has no trouble falling asleep at night and sometimes hears music while falling asleep. The patient's wife states that he typically sleeps soundly. He returned 2 weeks ago from a 15-day-long business trip to China. He is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (160 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m2. Blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg; remaining vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Results of sleep studies obtained 1 week later show the patient enters REM sleep within 5 minutes of two naps. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder that involves deficient orexin secretion, leading to the intrusion of sleep phenomena (eg, sleepiness, cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis) into times of wakefulness. The clinical diagnosis should be confirmed by a polysomnogram (to confirm the rapid transition into REM sleep and rule out other causes of daytime sleepiness) and a multiple sleep latency test (to confirm decreased sleep latency).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder characterized by daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, and sleep paralysis that responds to modafinil therapy. Narcolepsy arises from deficient production of orexins in the hypothalamus, which may result from genetic or autoimmune causes. Orexins typically induce the secretion of monoamines such as norepinephrine throughout the brain to promote wakefulness. As a result, patients suddenly and inappropriately transition into sleep during the day. Further, REM sleep features (eg, cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis) occur during wakefulness. The clinical diagnosis should bbe confirmed by a polysomnogram (to confirm the rapid transition into REM sleep and rule out other causes of daytime sleepiness) and a multiple sleep latency test (to confirm decreased sleep latency). Treatment includes a regular sleep schedule, avoidance of daytime naps, and, in patients with severe daytime sleepiness, 'modafinil. Modafinil has an unclear mechanism of action that involves increasing orexin and monoamine availabilty

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder (Choice A) refers to advanced or delayed sleep phase disorder. These circadian rhythm disorders do not feature decreased sleep latency or REM sleep phenomena during wakefulness. Insomnia disorder (Choice B) is unlikely as this patient was noted by his wife to sleep soundly. REM sleep behavior disorder (Choice D) features absent muscle atonia during REM sleep, and patients typically act out their dreams in a coordinated though 'sometimes violent manner. The intrusion of REM sleep phenomena into wakefulness would be atypical

questions.educationalObjective: Opiate intoxication causes symptoms of central nervous system (CNS) depression including sedation, respiratory depression, bradycardia, and hypotension. Miosis differentiates opiate intoxication from other CNS depressant toxidromes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient likely has opioid use disorder. Opioids are central nervous system (CNS) depressants used as analgesics and recreational drugs. Opiates act throughout the CNS and peripheral nervous system and interact with several neurotransmitter systems, leading to diverse effects. Opiate intoxication causes euphoria (due to interaction with dopamine), altered mental status, sedation, bradycardia and hypotension, depressed respiratory drive, and constricted pupils. Miosis is a distinctive finding that is less common in intoxication with other CNS depressants and is caused by direct opiate receptor activity in brain areas responsible for pupillary control. Opiates also act on receptors within the enteric nervous system, reducing gut motility and thus causing constipation.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: PCP (phencyclidine) use (Choices A, C, and E) is associated with aggression, rotatory nystagmus, and ataxia. It can also lead to depression, psychotic symptoms, memory loss, and dysarthria. Amphetamines (Choices D, E, and F) are CNS stimulants that cause euphoria and increase sympathetic tone, leading to tachycardia and hypertension, pupillary dilation, and restlessness (rather than sedation). Chronic amphetamine use can lead to amphetamine-induced psychotic disorder, which features chronic delusions, paranoia, and hallucinations. Chronic amphetamine use may also lead to cognitive impairment.

questions.retrievalTag: Anticholinergic toxicity can present as delirium sedation hallucinations ataxia visual
questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 30 minutes after her father found her lethargic and confused, with an empty bottle of "sleep aids" next to her. The patient has no history of serious illness, and her father was not aware that she was taking sleep medication. On arrival, the patient is agitated and uncooperative. She is not oriented to person, place, or time. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 128/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 138/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows 5-mm symmetric pupils and dry oral mucosa. The abdomen is soft and nontender; bowel sounds are decreased. Mental status examination shows decreased attention, intermittent somnolence, and agitation. She appears to be responding to internal stimuli and repeatedly screams at someone who is not in the room. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Anticholinergic toxicity can present as delirium, sedation, hallucinations, ataxia, visual disturbances, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, flushed skin, xerostomia, and mydriasis. Management includes discontinuation of the offending agent, supportive care with gentle sedation, fluids, and prevention of hyperthermia, seizure, or arrhythmias, and, in severe cases, treatment with pyridostigmine, a reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Acetylcholine activity in the brain mediates attention and motivation, and peripheral acetylcholine mediates smooth muscle contraction. Anticholinergic toxicity 'commonly causes delirium, sedation, and hallucinations, as observed in this patient, along with smooth muscle dysfunction (decreased function of salivary glands and pupillary muscles leading to this patient's dry oral mucosa and mydriasis, respectively). Additional manifestations include ataxia, visual disturbances (cyclopiegia, or loss of accommodation), tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. Management includes discontinuation of the offending agent, supportive care with gentle sedation, fluids, and prevention of hyperthermia, seizure, or arrhythmias, and, in severe cases, treatment with pyridostigmine, a reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), which are prescribed off-label as sleep aids, exert strong anticholinergic effects; patients 'must be monitored for cardiovascular toxicity (eg, conduction abnormalities such as atrioventricular block, QRS prolongation) as well. In patients with TCA toxicity and a QRS interval greater than 100 ms or a ventricular arrhythmia, sodium bicarbonate should be administered. Diphenhydramine, an antihistamine medication with strong anticholinergic activity, is also commonly used off-label as a sleep aid; toxicity presents similarly.

questions.retrievalTag: Obsessive-compulsive disorder is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions
questions.stem: Over the past 2 months, a 28-year-old mother has had recurrent thoughts about stabbing her 2-year-old daughter. She has a visual image of attacking her daughter which provokes extreme anxiety. She is horrified by these violent thoughts but cannot prevent them from happening. She says she would never harm her daughter. She believes that she is losing her mind. She has no history of violence and says that she has not been depressed. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (unwanted, intrusive thoughts that produce anxiety) that the patient may attempt to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). OCD is commonly treated with cognitive behavioral therapy and/or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive, anxiety-inducing urges or thoughts (obsessions) that the patient typically tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals (compulsions). Common obsessions are related to cleanliness, symmetry, harm to seif or others (as seen in this case), and taboo thoughts or ideas. Compulsions are rigidly applied repetitive behaviors (eg, checking, washing) or mental activities (eg, counting, repeating words) that relieve anxiety related to obsessions. Some patients report both obsessions and compulsions, though other patients experience either obsessions or compulsions only. 'These symptoms are time-consuming, cause significant anxiety to the patient, and markedly impair functioning. OCD is commonly treated with cognitive behavioral therapy as well as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors. Clomipramine and venlafaxine are second-line medication options.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Clozapine (Choice A) is an atypical antipsychotic medication used for treatment-refractory schizophrenia. It is ineffective in treating OCD. Imipramine (Choice C) is a tricyclic antidepressant without proven efficacy for management of OCD. It has an increased adverse-effect burden compared to SSRIs (eg, anticholinergic adverse effects). Itis not a firstline therapy for OCD. Lithium carbonate (Choice D) and valproic acid (Choice E) are mood stabilizing medications commonly used in treatment of bipolar disorder. They are not commonly used to treat OCD.

questions.retrievalTag: Defense mechanisms protect a person from conscious awareness of painful
questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman, who was diagnosed with asthma 16 years ago, comes to the office for a routine examination. The patient reports that her boyfriend of 2 years broke up with her 1 month ago. She was surprised and disappointed, but she has kept busy since that time by taking a pottery class, volunteering with a suicide hotline, and planning a trip. Medical history also is remarkable for an episode of major depressive disorder 3 years ago that was treated with sertraline; she stopped taking this medication 2 years ago. Her only current medication is beclomethasone; she took an oral contraceptive for 5 years but discontinued it 4 weeks ago because she is not interested in dating at this time. She appears cheerful. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. This patient's behavior is most likely indicative of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Defense mechanisms protect a person from conscious awareness of painful emotions, thoughts, or realities. Sublimation involves redirecting a difficult feeling or reality into a socially productive activity.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely sublimating her sadness by helping others in distress through her suicide hotline volunteering. Sublimation is a psychological defense 'mechanism that involves redirecting a difficult feeling into a socially productive activity. Itis considered an adaptive defense mechanism. When any person experiences painful thoughts, realities, or feelings such as anxiety or sadness, the mind has several unconscious methods of shielding the person from conscious, awareness of the thought, reality, or affect. These methods are called defense mechanisms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: In denial (Choice A), the mind protects itself from a painful external reality by unconsciously refusing to acknowledge it. For example, if an overweight man decides not to exercise because he denies he is overweight, his unconscious mind may be protecting him from the painful fact that he is overweight. This patient accepts that, her boyfriend has broken up with her. Hypomania (Choice B) is a mood episode characterized by a euphoric mood with increased goal-directed activity and may include the following: increased self- esteem, decreased sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, distractibility, and disinhibition or impulsivity. This patient is cheerful but does not demonstrate a decreased need for sleep, racing thoughts, distractibility, or disinhibition. She is engaged in socially appropriate activities to help her heal from her breakup. Projection (Choice C) is a psychological defense mechanism characterized by the misattribution of one's own thought or feeling to someone else. For example, an individual who is angry but cannot tolerate their own anger may instead believe their significant other is angry with them. Regression (Choice D) is a psychological defense mechanism in which individuals cope with difficult feelings by returning to a previous state of development. For instance, an adult patient receiving bad news may adopt the fetal position and cry.

questions.retrievalTag: Capacity assessments should be based on a discussion with the
questions.stem: A 63-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of an ulcer on his left foot that has been enlarging during the past 3 weeks. The patient has a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and is not adherent to his prescribed insulin regimen. He has no history of psychiatric illness. The patient is unable to provide information about his glucose concentrations at home. Evaluation confirms a foot infection, and amputation is recommended. The patient refuses the procedure, and a capacity evaluation is conducted. The patient reiterates that he does not want the amputation. The patient states that he has been told that the diabetes has led to an infection in the foot and that the blood flow is not strong enough to help his body fight the infection. He reports that he is receiving antibiotics and would like to continue them, even though the surgeons have told him that the chance for recovery is near zero. When asked what he thinks the most likely outcome of his choice will be, he responds, "I think the foot will get better. I have had diabetes a long time, and this has never happened before. I think the doctors are overreacting." Cognitive examination shows that he has intact attention, memory, and orientation to person, place, and time. Which of the following elements of capacity does this patient fail to demonstrate?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Appreciation of the consequences of his choice

questions.educationalObjective: Capacity assessments should be based on a discussion with the individual patient regarding the medical decision being made. To meet the criteria for decisional capacity, patients must express an appreciation of the consequences of their choice, communicate a consistent choice, illustrate an ability to reason through the options, and demonstrate an understanding of the relevant information and risks and benefits of the choice. The assessment must be performed rigorously in settings where the consequences of the decision are particularly serious.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Capacity assessments are based on an individualized discussion with the patient and can be conducted by any physician. To meet the criteria for decisional capacity, patients must express an appreciation of the consequences of their choice, communicate a consistent choice, illustrate an ability to reason through the options, and demonstrate an understanding of the relevant information and risks and benefits of the choice. Capacity is not a global assessment but rather pertains to a specific decision, and therefore the assessment should be based on the individual patient, their condition at the time, and the medical decision being made. The assessment must be performed rigorously in settings where the consequences of the decision are particularly serious, as in this case.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Communication of a choice (Choice B) is incorrect. This patient has repeatedly stated that he does not want an amputation, indicating that he can consistently 'communicate his choice. Reasoning about options (Choice C) is incorrect. He requests an alternative, non-operative treatment plan of continuing antibiotics, demonstrating an ability to reason through options. However, despite communicating understanding of the factors that make his chance of recovery with his chosen option near zero, the patient states that he believes the foot will get better and does not demonstrate an appreciation of the consequences of pursuing a likely futile treatment (worsening infection, sepsis, need for urgent amputation, and death). His explanation of the cause of his infection and reason his doctors do not think his body can fight it shows that he understands the relevant information (Choice D).

questions.retrievalTag: Acute lithium toxicity is characterized by neurologic  gastrointestinal and
questions.stem: A 57-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of moderate diarrhea and increasing clumsiness. He says he can no longer hold a coffee cup without spilling its contents. Four days ago, he saw a new physician, who started him on hydrochlorothiazide therapy for moderate hypertension, omeprazole for gastroesophageal reflux disease, lovastatin for hyperlipidemia, acetaminophen for back pain, and desloratadine for seasonal allergies. He has a 15-year history of bipolar disorder well controlled with lithium carbonate. He does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He does not appear dehydrated. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 140/105 mm Hg. Examination shows a fine tremor of both hands. His gait is mildly ataxic. On mental status examination, he is anxious but cooperative. He is oriented to person, place, and time. His serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference range. His serum lithium concentration is 1.8 mEq/L (therapeutic range = 0.6-1.2). Addition of which of the following medications most likely contributed to this patient's diarrhea and clumsiness?
questions.educationalObjective: Acute lithium toxicity is characterized by neurologic (eg, somnolence, confusion, ataxia, nystagmus, coarse tremors), gastrointestinal, and cardiac manifestations (eg, bradycardia). Because lithium is similar in charge and size to sodium and exclusively metabolized by the kidney, conditions that increase renal reabsorption of sodium or decrease renal function can increase serum lithium concentrations and the risk for lithium toxicity. Causes include thiazide diuretics, hypovolemia, NSAIDs, and ACE inhibitors.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: 'Acute lithium toxicity typically manifests with gastrointestinal (eg, nausea, vomiting, diarthea), cardiac (eg, QTe prolongation, bradycardia), and neurologic 'symptoms. Neurologic symptoms typically include somnolence, confusion, ataxia, and nystagmus, along with signs of neuromuscular excitability such as coarse tremors, fasciculations, and myoclonus. Severe toxicity leads to seizures. Lithium, an ion with approximately the same size and charge as sodium, is removed from Circulation by the kidney. Thus, conditions that change the excretion or reabsorption of sodium in the kidney can affect lithium concentrations as well. Thiazides <dieseuenentee haseiiinenn eaieietlinnt inn Gineiidiahdilenmmmnintianmaddalin: Redinnn ten lemeeen ail andiiemsacanee' Witeiions aealimenmniantitathen meiainaal annette tiaddie: Memeationnien circulation by the kidney. Thus, conditions that change the excretion or reabsorption of sodium in the kidney can affect lithium concentrations as well. Thiazides decrease sodium reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule, leading to increased sodium and lithium reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule. Hypovolemia (eg, from excessive exercise, heat, vomiting, or excessive diuresis) similarly promotes compensatory renal tubular sodium and lithium reabsorption. Another cause of increased serum lithium concentrations is decreased renal function. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) prevent prostaglandins from dilating the afferent arteriole, leading to a decreased glomerular fitration rate and consequent increases in serum lithium concentrations. Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors similarly decrease the glomerular filtration rate (by preventing the vasoconstriction of the efferent arteriole) and may increase serum lithium concentrations, 'Management of acute lithium toxicity requires fluid resuscitation and, in severe cases (eg, in patients with decreased consciousness, seizures, or serum lithium concentrations greater than 5 mEq/L), hemodialysis.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Acetaminophen (Choice A) is metabolized by the liver. At high doses, it can affect concentrations of drugs metabolized by the liver due to its effects on CYP enzymes or due to hepatotoxicity. It does not affect renal drug metabolism and is therefore a safe alternative to NSAIDs in patients who take lithium. Desloratadine (Choice B), an antihistamine, does not affect kidney function or renal clearance of sodium. It therefore does not have a significant effect on lithium concentrations. Lovastatin (Choice D), an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor, does not affect kidney function or renal clearance of sodium. It therefore does not have a significant effect 'on lithium concentrations. 'Omeprazole (Choice E), a proton pump inhibitor, does not affect kidney function or renal clearance of sodium. It therefore does not have a significant effect on lithium concentrations.

questions.retrievalTag: Normal aging includes a slow decline of executive functioning processing
questions.stem: An 87-year-old man comes to the office because of intermittent memory loss during the past 6 months. On several occasions, he has forgotten where he parked his car at the mall. Once, he eventually found the car while walking around; another time, a parking lot attendant drove him around until he found it. He has noticed no other changes in his functioning, but he is concerned because he prides himself on his ability to recall even insignificant details. He has migraines for which he takes intranasal sumatriptan as needed and ibuprofen. He appears to be in good health. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is pleasant and cooperative. His mood is anxious, and he has full range of affect. He is fully oriented. He recalls one of three words after 1 minute. After the examination is complete, he suddenly recalls the other two words. He says that this happens frequently, and it is his inability to retrieve memories on demand that is frustrating. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's memory loss?
questions.educationalObjective: Normal aging includes a slow decline of executive functioning, processing speed, episodic and working memory, attention span, and problem solving. No intervention is needed other than the encouragement of cognitive stimulation.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Normal aging includes a slow decline of executive functioning, processing speed, attention span, and problem solving, particularly after the age of 70 years. As well, aging people tend to struggle with simple forgetfulness (eg, misplacing the car in the parking lot) and some difficulty with episodic and working memory. A normally aging individual should still be able to function in society (eg, socialize, perform daily routines). Engaging in novel cognitive tasks (eg, learning a new language) may delay or slow these cognitive changes. Many aging patients worry that these findings reflect developing dementia; if there is clinical suspicion, neurocognitive screening should be performed; otherwise, reassurance is appropriate.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Dementia, Alzheimer type (Choice A) is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. This patient demonstrates cognitive changes consistent with normal aging. Dissociative amnesia (Choice B) refers to the inability to recall traumatic or anxiety-inducing events secondary to psychological distress. This patient's memory deficits are specific to individual details, not entire events. In addition, he has not recently experienced a traumatic event. lliness anxiety disorder (Choice C) is characterized by six months or more of excessive anxiety and preoccupation about the possibility of having or acquiring a serious illness such that patients perform frequent health-related behaviors (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor) or exhibit maladaptive avoidance (eg, completely avoiding going to the doctor). Cognitive concems are common in older patients, and this patient does not demonstrate maladaptive behaviors in response to his cognitive concern. Pseudodementia (Choice D) refers to cognitive impairment resulting from major depressive disorder. This patient does not demonstrate neurovegetative symptoms (eg, decreased energy or changes in appetite or sleep) or other depressive symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Patients should be emergently intubated and mechanically ventilated when unable
questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after her roommate found her in her dormitory room next to an empty unlabeled pill bottle. Administration of 100% oxygen by face mask and intravenous naloxone was begun en route to the hospital. On arrival, her temperature is 35.6°C (96°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 6/min and shallow, and blood pressure is 94/60 mm Hg. She is moderately responsive to painful stimuli. Her serum glucose concentration is 68 mg/dL. Arterial blood gas analysis shows: pH: 7.19, PCO2: 65 mm Hg, PO2: 108 mm Hg, HCO3: 20 mEq/L. Results of toxicology screening are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Delay treatment until results of toxicology screening are available

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Intravenous administration of 50% dextrose in water

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Administration of activated charcoal by nasogastric tube

questions.educationalObjective: Patients should be emergently intubated and mechanically ventilated when unable to maintain a patent airway or adequately oxygenate or ventilate as evidenced by clinical and laboratory evaluation. Intubation should precede further laboratory evaluation or treatment with specific antidotes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient demonstrates hypercapnic respiratory failure and severe mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis in the setting of drug overdose. Patients should be 'emergently intubated and mechanically ventilated when unable to maintain a patent airway or adequately oxygenate or ventilate as evidenced by clinical and laboratory evaluation. Intubation should be strongly considered with a respiratory rate above 30 breaths per minute, an inability to maintain arterial oxygen saturation of greater than 90% with Fic. greater than 0.60, serum pH less than 7.25, and Paco, greater than 50 mm Hg acutely. This patient meets two of these life-threatening criteria and should therefore be emergently intubated. After the patient has been stabilized, further history taking and toxicology screening can identify the ingested substance and guide further treatment. Given the patient's extremely low respiratory rate, opioid toxicity is likely. In severe opioid toxicity tissue hypoxia can lead to lactic acidosis and a mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis. Naloxone may not have fully displaced a high-affinity opioid receptor agonist such as fentanyl or, given naloxone's short duration of action, naloxone's effect may have worn off.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Delay treatment until results of toxicology screening are available (Choice A) is inappropriate given the patient's respiratory failure. Toxicology screening should follow intubation. Intravenous administration of 50% dextrose in water (Choice B) is the first management step in severe hypoglycemia. This mildly hypoglycemic patient's respiratory failure is more urgent to address. Intravenous administration of sodium bicarbonate (Choice C) addresses some toxic ingestions (eg, sodium channel blockers, salicylates, methanol, ethylene glycol). 'Administration of activated charcoal by nasogastric tube (Choice D) is a common treatment strategy after recent acetaminophen ingestion. However, regardless of the ingested substance, the patient's respiratory failure should be addressed first.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician 2 days after being treated in the emergency department for his third episode of chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, and fear of dying over the past 2 weeks. Examination shows normal findings. Alprazolam therapy is initiated, and a follow-up examination is scheduled. This patient should be monitored for which of the following at his return visit?
questions.educationalObjective: Alprazolam is a fast-acting benzodiazepine that may be utilized in the acute setting to extinguish anxiety. Given its short half-life, alprazolam is associated with rebound anxiety and withdrawal symptoms (eg, sympathetic hyperactivity). Benzodiazepines impair learning and anxiety habituation in psychotherapy and should therefore be avoided as a long-term treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alprazolam is a fast-acting benzodiazepine that may be utlized in the acute setting to extinguish anxiety. Benzodiazepines are allosteric modulators of the y- 'aminobutyric acid A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx in neurons in the amygdala and thus decreasing neuronal fring. Given its short half-life, alprazolam is associated with rebound anxiety and withdrawal symptoms (eg, sympathetic hyperarousal). Switching to a long-acting benzodiazepine such as clonazepam can reduce these risks. Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with symptoms of sympathetic hyperactivity such as tachycardia, heart palpitations, sweating, and trembling, along with shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, gastrointestinal distress, and paresthesias. Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Physical examination may demonstrate signs of sympathetic hyperarousal (eg, anxious affect, tremuulousness, and diaphoresis) during the panic attack but is typically normal after panic attack resolution. Treatment typically includes psychotherapy, long-term selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and short-term benzodiazepines. explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Agranulocytosis (Choice A) is a rare but serious adverse effect of clozapine. Benzodiazepines are not associated with agranulocytosis. 'Akathisia (Choice B) is a neuropsychiatric syndrome featuring inner restlessness that may be associated with intense anxiety and a compulsion to move. It is associated with the antidopaminergic activity of antipsychotic medications, not benzodiazepines, which may be used to treat akathisia. COrthostatic hypotension (Choice C) is associated with a-1 adrenergic antagonism. Some anti-anxiety medications such as prazosin and clonidine antagonize the a-1 adrenergic receptor, but benzodiazepines do not. Urinary retention (Choice E) is associated with anticholinergic activity. Several psychotropic medications are associated with anticholinergic adverse effects, including atypical antipsychotics and low-potency typical antipsychotics. Benzodiazepines do not demonstrate significant anticholinergic activity.

questions.retrievalTag: In post-traumatic stress disorder  patients experience intrusive memories hyperarousal
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his social worker 3 days after being placed in his fourth foster home in the past year. His social worker states that his behavior is making it difficult to find foster care placements for the boy, and he is at risk for placement in a group home. In each home, he becomes easily frustrated, aggressive, and is disrespectful. He has hit and injured younger children in some of the homes. He does not sleep well, cries out at night, and has nightmares. He refuses to go to school and sometimes hides under his bed. The social worker does not know details about his early life except that both of his parents were active substance abusers, and he and his older sister were found in an abandoned apartment with no heat, water, or food when he was 4 years old. He is at the 40th percentile for height and the 30th percentile for weight. Physical examination shows several healing abrasions over both knees. On mental status examination, he is irritable, easily frustrated, and impulsive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: In post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), patients experience intrusive memories, hyperarousal, avoidance, and negative mood/thoughts that endure for longer than one month secondary to a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. Psychotherapy and/or antidepressant medication can address PTSD symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a syndrome of psychological consequences of direct or witnessed exposure to serious physical or sexual violence or trauma, Though some patients recover quickly from traumatic events, vulnerable patients may develop PTSD. Given his other PTSD symptoms and trauma history, it is more likely this child's aggression is caused by PTSD rather than oppositional defiant disorder. Separation anxiety disorder (Choice D) refers to excessive fear of separation from an attachment figure. This patient does not appear to have attachment figures currently (likely a consequence of PTSD). 'Social anxiety disorder (social phobia) (Choice E) refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social and/or performance situations leading to significant distress andlor impaired functioning. Given his other PTSD symptoms and trauma history, this patient's anxiety about going to school is more likely secondary to PTSD.

questions.retrievalTag: When a patient lacks decision-making capacity healthcare proxies next of
questions.stem: A 94-year-old woman is brought to the office by her daughter for a follow-up examination. Three days ago, the patient was diagnosed with a urinary tract infection and prescribed oral antibiotics, which she has refused to take. She also has refused to eat and has been drinking minimal fluids during this time. She has dementia, Alzheimer type and lives in a nursing care facility. For the past 3 months, she has been unable to ambulate, get dressed, or bathe without assistance. Her verbal communication is limited to one-word answers. The patient's daughter has paperwork indicating that she is her mother's healthcare proxy; she says that her mother does not want extraordinary life-sustaining measures, including parenteral nutrition and hydration. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Intravenous administration of fluids and antibiotics

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Nasogastric administration of fluids and antibiotics

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Periodically offering semi-solid food by mouth

questions.educationalObjective: When a patient lacks decision-making capacity, healthcare proxies, next of kin, or advance directives should be consulted to assist with end-of-life decisions such as initiating comfort care, which focuses on decreasing suffering. Comfort care avoids invasive procedures and allows patients the opportunity to take food by mouth.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: 'According to the ethical principle of autonomy, a patient's wishes about end-of-life care should be respected. There are several methods to determine a patient's, wishes, including communicating with the patient directly if the patient has decision-making capacity, reviewing advance directives, consulting durable powers of attorney or healthcare proxies, and/or consulting with close family members. This patient only has the cognitive capacity to provide one-word answers and therefore does not appear to possess decision-making capacity; she cannot express a consistent choice, demonstrate an understanding of the risks and benefits of the choice, demonstrate an appreciation of the significance of the choice for her personal circumstances, or illustrate an ability to reason through the options. Therefore, the medical team should defer to this patient's healthcare proxy. A patient's healthcare proxy makes decisions based on their knowledge of what the patient would have wanted when the patient possessed decision-making capacity. This patient's healthcare proxy indicates that the patient prefers comfort care, which indicates that the goals of care are to decrease suffering rather than prolong life. Therefore, invasive or diagnostic procedures should be avoided. Taking food by mouth is a basic human comfort and the least invasive method of providing nutrition, so the medical team should periodically offer semi-solid food by mouth.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Intravenous administration of fluids and antibiotics (Choice A), nasogastric administration of fluids and antibiotics (Choice C), and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (Choice D) are invasive methods for administering nutrition, These procedures violate comfort care principles. MRI of the brain (Choice B) can help elucidate the cause of a patient's cognitive impairment. This patient has already been diagnosed with dementia, Alzheimer type. As well, her healthcare proxy has activated comfort care; therefore, diagnostic testing should be avoided.

questions.retrievalTag: Functional neurologic disorder involves neurologic symptoms such as sensory or
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband after the sudden onset of blindness. She has been under the care of a psychiatrist for the past 18 months and has been treated with clonazepam and fluoxetine. She has had no changes in sleep or appetite. Yesterday, she witnessed an elderly woman being struck and killed by a bus. On physical examination, the pupils are equal and reactive to light. There is bilateral nystagmus when the physician moves a strip of paper with vertical stripes in front of her eyes. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. She has an unsteady gait and takes small steps with her arms outstretched. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Functional neurologic disorder involves neurologic symptoms such as sensory or motor dysfunction that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. Functional neurologic symptom disorder is postulated to represent the subconscious conversion of intolerable emotional distress into neurologic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Functional neurologic symptom disorder involves neurologic symptoms such as sensory or motor dysfunction that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. In patients with ophthalmologic causes of vision loss, optokinetic stimulation (showing vertical stripes, which simulates motion in the visual fields) does not typically produce nystagmus. This patient's bilateral nystagmus with optokinetic stimulation suggests a top-down, or psychogenic, cause of blindness. Though the symptoms disrupt daily life, patients with functional neurologic symptom disorder may or may not be preoccupied with their symptoms. Functional neurologic symptom disorder is thought to represent the subconscious conversion of intolerable emotional distress (eg, this patient's distress about witnessing a death) into neurologic symptoms. Psychiatric comorbidities such as depression and anxiety are common but not present in all patients. Physicians should conduct a thorough history and physical and obtain laboratory and imaging studies to evaluate for medical or neurologic disease that may explain or partially 'explain the symptoms when the diagnosis is unclear. Treatment of functional neurologic symptom disorder involves education about the diagnosis, physical therapy, and cognitive behavioral therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate psychological distress within three months of an identifiable stressor. This patient's psychological distress has been converted into a blindness, which is more consistent with functional neurologic symptom disorder. 'Acute stress disorder (Choice B) presents similarly to post-traumatic stress disorder, with symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance, and/or negative 'moodithoughts within one month of a traumatic event. Though this patient has experienced a traumatic event, she does not report these psychological symptoms. In factitious disorder (Choice C), patients consciously, or purposely, produce symptoms (eg, purposely injure themselves, fake a fever by eating something warm) for primary gain. A history of multiple procedures and subjective or objective findings that are inconsistent with known disease suggests factitious disorder. This patient's neurologic symptoms are unconsciously produced. In somatic symptom disorder (Choice E), the patient is excessively and persistently preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. If somatic symptoms are neurologic in nature, functional neurologic symptom disorder is a more precise diagnosis than somatic symptom disorder (functional neurologic symptom disorder describes a specific subset of patients who are preoccupied with physical symptoms).

questions.retrievalTag: Drug-induced acute pancreatitis is a rare adverse effect of valproic
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 4-hour history of worsening abdominal pain. He also has felt nauseated and "bloated" for the past day, and during the past 2 hours, he has had three episodes of emesis. The patient has bipolar disorder and has been treated with valproic acid for the past 4 years. His only other medication is ibuprofen for pain as needed. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years and drinks ten glasses of wine weekly. He does not use illicit drugs. His diet consists primarily of fast food. The patient appears very uncomfortable and is curled into a ball on the examination table. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg. Physical examination shows diaphoresis. There is significant tenderness to palpation of the middle and upper left abdomen. Results of laboratory studies are pending. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Drug-induced acute pancreatitis is a rare adverse effect of valproic acid that is idiosyncratic (not related to drug dosage or duration of treatment). The mechanism is unknown. In most cases, the pancreatitis is self-limited and resolves with discontinuation of the drug.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: 'Acute pancreatitis presents with epigastric abdominal pain, nausea, and emesis. Itis diagnosed when patients meet at least two of three criteria: epigastric pain that radiates to the back, lipase concentration at least three times the upper limit of normal, and/or evidence of acute pancreatitis on abdominal imaging. The most 'common causes of acute pancreatitis are alcohol use and gallstones. Less common causes include trauma, hypertriglyceridemia, hypercalcemia, and adverse medication effects. Valproic acid is an anti-epileptic drug and mood stabilizer that is used to treat patients with schizoaffective or bipolar disorder. Drug-induced acute pancreatitis is a rare adverse effect of valproic acid that is idiosyncratic (not related to drug dosage or duration of treatment). The mechanism of this adverse reaction is unknown but is possibly related to the accumulation over time of reactive oxygen species-induced endothelial damage within the pancreas. In patients in whom valproic acid-induced pancreatitis is suspected, other possible causes of acute pancreatitis should be excluded, In most cases, the pancreatitis is self-limited and resolves with discontinuation of the drug. Other idiosyncratic valproic acid reactions include hepatotoxicity, bone marrow toxicity, and alopecia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Alcohol consumption (Choice A) is a common cause of acute pancreatitis. However, alcohol use is an unlikely cause of acute pancreatitis in this patient who consumes below the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's recommended weekly maximum of 14 alcoholic drinks. Cigarette smoking (Choice B) could cause pancreatic cancer, a rare cause of acute pancreatitis. Pancreatic cancer typically presents in patients older than age 40 years with unexplained weight loss preceding acute symptoms. Fast food consumption (Choice C) may lead to hypertriglyceridemia, which can lead to acute pancreatitis. However, this patient does not have a history of obesity, diabetes mellitus, a disorder of lipoprotein metabolism, or other risk factors for hypertriglyceridemia-induced acute pancreatitis. Ibuprofen (Choice D) is not typically associated with acute pancreatitis.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police because of extreme confusion and unintelligible communication; he was found wandering in a street. On arrival, he requires restraints for combativeness. He repeatedly yells, "Get them off me!" He is uncooperative and unable to give any history. His temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 112/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 156/104 mm Hg. Examination shows a thin, disheveled man with normal pupils. There is palmar erythema and gynecomastia. Mental status examination shows he is not oriented to person, place, or time, and he refers to the physicians as sailors. Serum studies show a glucose concentration of 72 mg/dL total protein concentration of 5.5 g/dL and albumin concentration of 3.0 g/dL Toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium tremens is a life-threatening complication of alcohol withdrawal that presents with severe confusion, agitation, visual hallucinations, and autonomic instability.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely developing delirium tremens, a serious complication of alcohol withdrawal. Alcohol increases the central nervous system activity of y- aminobutyric acid, the main inhibitory neurotransmitter, agonism of which is counterbalanced by upregulation of native central nervous system excitatory pathways (eg, glutamate). Signs of chronic alcohol use include gynecomastia and palmar erythema. Abrupt discontinuation of alcohol leads to excitatory-inhibitory imbalance with net function resulting in excess central nervous system excitation and sympathetic nervous system activity. Uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal typically presents with tremors, anxiety, restlessness, headache, nausea, and diaphoresis, which can occur hours after the cessation of alcohol use. Uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal typically presents with tremors, anxiety, restlessness, headache, nausea, and diaphoresis, which can occur hours after the cessation of alcohol use. Delirium tremens, the most concerning and life-threatening complication of alcohol withdrawal, involves severe confusion and disorientation, fluctuations in consciousness, agitation, visual hallucinations, and autonomic instability, such as in this patient. Patients with delirium tremens may experience life-threatening dysrhythmias of respiratory failure. Delirium tremens is managed supportively with benzodiazepines to address agitation and prevent the symptoms of withdrawal, IV fluids, nutritional supplementation, and frequent assessment including vital sign checks. Other complications of alcohol withdrawal include alcoholic hallucinosis (auditory or visual hallucinations without confusion or autonomic instability) and seizures.

questions.retrievalTag: Hospice care is a model of health care that prioritizes
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with widely metastatic lung cancer is admitted to the hospice unit. He has documented metastatic disease to liver and bone, and it has been determined that further attempts to treat his cancer would be futile. The patient has a history of hepatitis C and intravenous drug use; he reports last using heroin 6 months ago. Treatment of severe bone pain with fentanyl patches has not been effective. The patient is alert, fully oriented, and appears uncomfortable. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach to pain management for this patient?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Add short-acting opioids for breakthrough pain to the current regimen

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Continue to use only long-acting opioid medications for pain

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Discontinue fentanyl, initiate methadone, and avoid short-acting opioids

questions.educationalObjective: Hospice care is a model of health care that prioritizes quality of life over prolongation of life. According to this model, patients with substance use disorder should be provided with the most effective options for pain management, including short-acting opioids.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient has a terminal cancer diagnosis causing pain that is significantly impacting his quality of life. Hospice care is a model of health care that prioritizes quality of life over prolongation of life, emphasizing the palliation of physical, emotional, and spiritual pain. Patients with terminal illnesses and substance use disorders should be offered hospice care according to the ethical principles of beneficence (patients benefit from pain relief), nonmaleficence (psychological and spiritual harms are decreased when pain is managed), and autonomy (pain decreases patients' ability to exercise their autonomy). The gold standard of pain 'management involves adding short-acting opioids for breakthrough pain when scheduled opioids do not sufficiently manage pain. Short-acting opioids are thought to be safe if used properly by patients with life-limiting illness.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Add buprenorphine for breakthrough pain (Choice A), continue to use only long-acting opioid medications for pain (Choice C), discontinue fentanyl and initiate buprenorphine (Choice D), and discontinue fentanyl, initiate methadone, and avoid short-acting opioids (Choice €) are unlikely to fully address this patient's pain Buprenorphine and methadone are utilized for maintenance treatment or opioid use disorder but are less effective than short-acting opioids for pain. These options are inconsistent with the hospice care model.

She reports poor sleep and believes that her telephone is being tapped and has refused to interact with her family. She reports hearing voices and believes she is being plotted against. Medications include haloperidol. She has a history of alcohol use disorder and was hospitalized for psychiatric symptoms 30 years ago, but records are unavailable. She appears agitated and irritable. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a neutral mood and auditory hallucinations. Her insight and judgment are poor. An MRI of the brain shows age-related changes but is otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.educationalObjective: Schizophrenia should be at the top of the differential diagnosis in elderly patients with evidence of chronic and persistent psychotic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient demonstrates psychotic symptoms (eg, auditory hallucinations, persecutory delusions) and consequent irritability and agitation. Whereas new-onset and progressive psychotic symptoms in an elderly patient should prompt evaluation for dementia, this patient demonstrates evidence of chronic psychotic symptoms as demonstrated by the persistence of symptoms during her year of nursing home residence and a history of psychiatric hospitalization.
