questions.retrievalTag: Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine examination. During the interview, she says she has had a lack of energy, occasional anxiety, and difficulty sleeping during the past 2 to 3 years. She says she no longer takes pleasure in activities that she used to enjoy. She smokes marijuana twice monthly but does not use any other illicit drugs. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her pulse is 68/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory: TSH 3.5, T4 5. Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms (including depressed mood during more days than not) that have endured for at least two years. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Persistent depressive disorder, previously called dysthymia, refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least two years. Patients must report a depressed mood on more days than not. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness. This patient also demonstrates anhedonia as evidenced by a lack of enjoyment in previously enjoyable activities; however, she does not have at least five symptoms of major depressive disorder, making persistent depressive disorder the most accurate diagnosis. Emerging evidence indicates that persistent depressive disorder should be managed similarly to major depressive disorder: antidepressant medication plus psychotherapy is most effective. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are generally the first-line medications given their low adverse-effect burden.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice A) is an anxiety disorder characterized by six months or more excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient reports occasional anxiety only. Hypothyroidism (Choice B) can present with depressive symptoms. However, in addition to depression symptoms, patients with hypothyroidism typically demonstrate other physical examination findings (eg, dry skin, pretibial myxedema, coarse hair). This patient does not demonstrate any of these findings, and her serum thyroid-stimulating hormone and thyroxine (T4) concentrations are within the reference ranges. Major depressive disorder (Choice C) includes two or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). This patient reports fewer than five depressive symptoms. In addition, major depressive episodes are typically shorter-lived than this patient's depressive symptoms. Substance-induced depressive disorder (Choice E) is unlikely given this patient's negative urine toxicology screen and intermittent, limited use of marijuana.

The patient reports that he feels "thirsty all the time," and has experienced frequent urination during the past 6 months. He does not find these symptoms overly bothersome and states that he drinks "a lot of water during the day." He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hepatitis C, and bipolar disorder. Medications are metoprolol, metformin, atorvastatin, and lithium carbonate. Vital signs are within normal limits, and physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a euthymic mood. Results of laboratory studies show: Hgb A1c 5.9%, BUN 15, glucose fasting 85, creatinine 0.9, Na 140, Lithium 0.9. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in addressing this patient's symptoms?

questions.educationalObjective: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is a known adverse effect of lithium. Patients present with polydipsia, polyuria, concentrated serum, and dilute urine. hypernatremia lithium discontinuation should be considered. However, in the absence of hypernatremia, patients can continue lithium and should be encouraged to maintain water intake.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely experiencing lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI). Lithium is a mood stabilizing medication utilized to treat acute mania and as maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder. At therapeutic doses, lithium can cause a variety of adverse effects, including tremor, nystagmus, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and thyroid dysfunction. Lithium concentrates within the cells of the renal collecting duct and interferes with the signaling pathway related to anti-diuretic hormone (ADH), leading to limited aquaporin insertion and resultant NDI. Normally, ADH triggers insertion of aquaporin channels into the collecting duct. When this process fails (as in NDI) the ability of the kidney to reclaim free water is compromised. Dehydration results, with symptoms of polydipsia, a normal compensatory Tesponse to low serum volume or increased serum osmolarity, and polyuria, as a result of the large volume of dilute urine produced. Laboratory evaluation reveals increased serum osmolarity and decreased urine osmolarity and, in severe cases, hypernatremia (in most cases, the thirst mechanism appropriately regulates sodium). Itis recommended that patients without hypernatremia maintain their water intake to prevent dehydration and the development of hypernatremia, along with a low-salt and low-sodium diet in some patients. Patients who develop hypematremia should be managed according to the typical hypernatremia treatment algorithm and lithium discontinuation should be strongly considered,

However, this patient is most likely experiencing NDI. Fluid restriction increases the risk for hypernatremia and would be inappropriate for this patient.

questions.retrievalTag: Cocaine intoxication is associated with seizures
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by friends after having a brief generalized tonic-clonic seizure. She is alert and restless and complains of a generalized headache. Her pulse is 110/min. She has signs of mild rhinitis and a small laceration of the tongue. Which of the following is the most likely substance taken?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine intoxication is associated with seizures. Signs of cocaine intoxication include tachycardia, hypertension, and pupillary dilation. Chronic cocaine abuse leads to otolaryngologic symptoms such as rhinitis and nasal septal perforation.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Cocaine is an addictive recreational drug that typically causes euphoria, restlessness, and increased sympathetic tone (tachycardia, hypertension, pupillary dilation). 'Complications of cocaine intoxication can include vasospasm, myocardial infarction, and seizures. Cocaine blocks the presynaptic reuptake of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, which increases the concentration of these substances in the synaptic cleft. Increased synaptic serotonin leads to euphoria, while increased 'synaptic dopamine leads to addiction (and in severe cases, hallucinations and paranoia), and increased synaptic norepinephrine can cause tachycardia and hypertension through activation of sympathetic adrenoreceptors leading to increased cardiac chronotropy and peripheral vascular vasoconstriction. Chronic cocaine abuse may lead to otolaryngologic symptoms such as rhinitis, nasal septal perforation, and nosebleeds as well as neuropsychiatric symptoms such as anosmia, cognitive impairment, and psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Alcohol (Choice A) is a central nervous system (CNS) depressant that can cause slurred speech, emotional lability, behavioral disinhibition, ataxia, and memory lapses. Barbiturates (Choice B) are also CNS depressants that have similar effects in toxicity to alcohol and benzodiazepines. Alcohol and barbiturate withdrawal, not intoxication, can lead to seizures. Heroin (Choice D) intoxication causes euphoria, altered mental status, sedation, bradycardia and hypotension, depressed respiratory drive (or apnea), and constricted pupils. Seizures would be atypical. LSD (Choice E) is a potent hallucinogen known to cause auditory and visual hallucinations, depersonalization, flashbacks, and paranoia. Marijuana (Choice F) is a hallucinogen that causes euphoria, paranoia, hallucinations, cognitive slowing, conjunctival injection, and dilated pupils. Phencyclidine (PCP) (Choice G) can cause similar symptoms to LSD and is also associated with aggression, rotatory nystagmus, and ataxia,

questions.retrievalTag: Bulimia nervosa involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital through the emergency department (ED) because of a 3-day history of weakness, fatigue, and occasional palpitations. In the ED, serum potassium concentration was 2.1 mEq/L. The patient says she is dissatisfied with her body image, leading her to periodically purge and secretly take her mother's hydrochlorothiazide. Her mood has become increasingly depressed since a long-term relationship ended 2 months ago. The patient appears weary but makes eye contact. She is 167 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (161 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Which of the following medications is contraindicated for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Bulimia nervosa (binging/purging type) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxative, or diuretic overuse. Electrolyte disturbances can increase seizure risk; thus, bupropion is contraindicated in patients with bulimia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bulimia nervosa (binging/purging type) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, laxative, or diuretic overuse that occur atleast once a week for three months or more, Hypokalemia is common with repeated vomiting, as there are direct gastrointestinal losses via emesis, and increased bicarbonate leads to renal proton-potassium exchange with consequent potassium excretion. Hypokalemia would also be expected with apn cst sagerabecencysthpeaimndaibtnpldg tame eceineh Mirtrialpstes ented arora tranentcentel..geenrarsssotnbiparte te? digested A load to the collecting duct and promoting potassium excretion. In severe cases of bulimia nervosa, signs of hypovolemia such as tachycardia and hypotension may be present. Hypovolemia leads to increased aldosterone concentrations, resulting in further potassium secretion. As potassium is involved in the membrane potential of muscle cells (including skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscles), hypokalemia typically leads to general weakness, muscle cramps, fatigue, palpitations, decreased gastrointestinal motility, and/or cardiac arrhythmia if severe. Hypovolemia also increases antidiuretic hormone release, increasing free water retention and promoting hyponatremia. Hypocalcemia can also occur in bulimia nervosa, though hydrochlorothiazide misuse can alternatively lead to hypercalcemia. These electrolyte changes, particularly sodium and calcium derangements, can decrease patients' seizure threshold. As such, bupropion, an antidepressant medication that increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine and decreases the seizure threshold (through a mechanism not fully elucidated that may be related to sympathomimetic activity), is contraindicated. Medications that significantly prolong the QTc interval are also relatively contraindicated because they may increase patients' arrhythmia tisk.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Clonazepam (Choice B) is a benzodiazepine medication utilized to treat anxiety. It decreases seizure risk and is therefore not contraindicated in patients with bulimia nervosa. Desvenlafaxine (Choice C) is the active metabolite of venlafaxine and acts in a pharmacologically equivalent way to venlafaxine as an antidepressant, increasing synaptic serotonin and norepinephrine. Desveniafaxine is not highly associated with seizure risk or QTc prolongation, Escitalopram (Choice D) is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor antidepressant commonly utlized to treat depression and anxiety. It has only minor effects on QTc and seizure threshold. Selegiline (Choice E) is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAO!) antidepressant medication utilized in depression and Parkinson disease. Tyramine-containing foods should be avoided in patients on MAOIs because of the risk for hypertensive crisis. However, selegiline has not been associated with significant seizure or QTc prolongation risk.

questions.retrievalTag: Though patients may make decisions that physicians deem as poor
questions.stem: A 20-year-old man comes to the physician at the request of his parents. He was recently expelled from college for repeatedly violating dormitory rules. He is currently not working and does not have a permanent residence. He drinks three beers nightly and smokes marijuana occasionally. On mental status examination, he is irritable. There is no evidence of depression, psychosis, or suicidal or homicidal ideation. During the interview, he says that he does not want any sort of treatment or to come back to the clinic and does not want his parents contacted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Explain to him that if he does not cooperate, his parents can have him admitted to the psychiatric unit

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Schedule a family meeting and tell the patient you expect to see him there

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Tell the patient you will arrange for residential substance abuse treatment

questions.educationalObjective: Though patients may make decisions that physicians deem as poor medical judgment, the ethical principle of autonomy dictates that patients have the right to make these decisions. Coercive treatment is only warranted when patients experience medical or psychiatric emergencies that interfere with their ability to make decisions.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Physicians should respect patient autonomy. This principle is especially pertinent when the patient is an adult without evidence of psychiatric or medical conditions that impair basic decision-making capabilities. Though patients may make decisions physicians deem as poor medical judgment, the ethical principle of autonomy dictates that patients have the right to make these decisions. Coercive treatment is only warranted when patients experience medical or psychiatric conditions that interfere with their ability to make decisions. For example, a patient experiencing acute psychosis who expresses homicidal ideation with a plan (a decision he would not normally make if his psychosis were treated), would require involuntary psychiatric hospitalization. However, this patient denies safety concems and should not receive coercive treatment. Ifthe patient consents to continued conversation with the physician, the physician can attempt motivational interviewing to address his unhealthy behaviors

'Schedule a family meeting and tell the patient you expect to see him there (Choice C), schedule individual psychotherapy (Choice D), and tell the patient you will arrange for residential substance abuse treatment (Choice E) would violate the patient's autonomy, as he expressed that he did not want medical treatment.

questions.retrievalTag: Delusional disorder is a psychotic disorder that features the presence
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of a recent episode of disruptive behavior. He works as a software engineer. He says that the company is constantly spying on him. He states that company operatives are opening and reading all of his mail, that his phone is bugged at work and at home, and that hidden cameras are watching his every move. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. He says he has not had hallucinations, depressed mood, difficulty sleeping, or decreased appetite. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delusional disorder is a psychotic disorder that features the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. Treatment commonly includes antipsychotic medication and psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Delusional disorder is a psychotic disorder that features the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms (eg, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, disorganized behaviors other than behaviors related to the delusion), or mood symptoms. Persecutory delusions (as in this patient) and somatic delusions are common types of delusions. Many patients will only present for care at the insistence of other people given their lack of insight into the psychotic nature of their concem. Treatment typically includes antipsychotic medication and psychotherapy with the goal of improving the patient's coping with the delusion and gradually increasing insight into the delusion. The prognosis is guarded,

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Bipolar disorder (Choice A) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can sometimes be accompanied by psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. This patient does not report mood symptoms. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice C) is an anxiety disorder that presents with excessive and difficult to control worrying, hyperactivity, disordered sleep, fatigue, and trouble relaxing for at least six months. This patient's anxiety is specific to his persecutory delusion. Major depressive disorder (Choice D) includes two or more weeks of depressive symptoms and if severe may include mood congruent delusions (eg, delusions of Quilt). This patient does not report mood symptoms and his delusions are not depressive in nature. 'Schizophrenia (Choice E) is a chronic psychotic disorder characterized by delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and negative symptoms (eg, alogia, apathy, flattened affect). This patient's presentation does not include hallucinations, disorganized thinking, or disorganized behavior (other than behaviors related to his specific delusion).

questions.retrievalTag: Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood
questions.stem: A 27-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks' gestation is brought to the emergency department by her husband because of bizarre behavior. He reports that during the past week, she has been sleeping only 4 to 5 hours nightly and has not slept at all for the past 2 days. She spends most of her time talking on the telephone and this morning began talking about having a special mission. She has no history of psychiatric illness. She is alert and oriented. Her speech is rapid and labile. She says she hears the voice of God speaking to her and describes receiving a special mission to end world poverty. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Electroconvulsive therapy is a safe and effective option to treat mania in pregnant patients.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is experiencing an index episode of acute mania and can therefore be diagnosed with bipolar disorder. Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of mania and may include episodes of depression. Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, a decreased heed for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Some patients demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, grandiose delusions as in this patient, hallucinations). The postpartum period is a high-risk time for mania caused by hormonal changes and sleep deprivation, though patients in the antepartum period possess similar risk to non-pregnant patients. Management of acute mania typically requires mood stabilizing therapy (eg, mood stabilizers or mood stabilizing atypical antipsychotics). However, many mood stabilizing agents possess teratogenic properties and should be avoided during pregnancy. Of the options provided, electroconvulsive therapy is the safest and most effective for pregnant patients, Certain atypical antipsychotics are considered relatively safe and effective for treating mania in pregnancy as well.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Carbamazepine therapy (Choice A) is a mood stabilizer that can be utilized to treat acute mania in non-pregnant patients. However, carbamazepine administration during pregnancy can lead to developmental delays, abnormal IQ, and growth retardation in infants, so would be inappropriate for this patient. Cognitive behavioral therapy (Choice B) and supportive psychotherapy (Choice D) are unlikely to be effective for acute mania, as patients' distractibility, increased goal-directed activity, and delusional thinking patterns preclude meaningful engagement with a therapist. Once patients with bipolar disorder are euthymic, then adjunctive psychotherapy that incorporates psychoeducation (including psychoeducation of family members) can be incorporated into the treatment plan. Valproic acid therapy (Choice E) is a mood stabilizer that is frequently utilized to treat acute mania in non-pregnant patients. However, valproic acid therapy is associated with neural tube defects, especially when utilized in the first trimester.

questions.retrievalTag: Female orgasmic disorder is characterized by a decreased frequency or
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of an inability to reach orgasm. She says that while her partner has been patient and does not pressure her, she is concerned that he will eventually break up with her. She has no significant medical history. She has taken drospirenone-ethinyl estradiol for the past 4 years. When asked about her sexual history, she describes a previous boyfriend who "coerced her into some experimentation that she did not enjoy." She drinks four to five 12-oz beers on weekends. On mental status examination, she describes her mood as "frustrated," and her affect is tearful. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Female orgasmic disorder is characterized by a decreased frequency or intensity of orgasm with associated distress or interpersonal difficulty. Historical relationship trauma, current relationship stress, and psychological factors (eg, performance anxiety) are risk factors.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely experiencing female orgasmic disorder, which is characterized by a decreased frequency or intensity of orgasm with associated distress or interpersonal difficulty. Risk factors include historical factors (eg, a history of sexual assault), psychological factors (eg, a tendency toward behavioral inhibition, performance anxiety), relationship factors (eg, mistrust within the relationship), and cultural factors (eg, negative cultural attitudes toward female sexuality). An association may exist between female orgasmic disorder and psychiatric disorders such as depression or anxiety. Initial management includes patient education and directed masturbation (having the patient practice reaching climax alone then incorporating the sexual partner). If these methods are ineffective, then cognitive behavioral therapy can be helpful. explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: An adverse effect of drospirenone-ethinyl estradiol (Choice A) is an unlikely explanation for this patient's sexual dysfunction. This medication has not been associated with sexual dysfunction, and this patient has several psychological and relationship factors that better explain her sexual dysfunction. Alcohol use disorder (Choice B) is unlikely. Though alcohol can inhibit sexual function, this patient only consumes moderate amounts of alcohol. Major depressive disorder (Choice D) presents with two or more weeks of depressive symptoms and associated changes in energy, sleep, and appetite, among other symptoms. Though this patient is tearful, she does not report depressive symptoms. Her tears likely reflect her distress about her sexual dysfunction rather than a persistently depressed mood. In post-traumatic stress disorder (Choice ), patients experience intrusive memories, hyperarousal, avoidance, and negative moodithoughts that endure for longer than one month secondary to a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. Though post-traumatic stress disorder is associated with sexual dysfunction, this patient does not demonstrate these symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Opiate withdrawal has symptoms common to other CNS depressants such
questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of generalized muscle and joint pain, nausea, and abdominal cramping for 36 hours. She has a history of chronic pelvic pain and has seen several different physicians about her condition. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Examination shows excessive tearing, rhinorrhea, and piloerection. The most likely cause of these symptoms is withdrawal from which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Opiate withdrawal has symptoms common to other CNS depressants such as restlessness, tachycardia, hypertension, tremors, nausea, and vomiting. Lacrimation, rhinorrhea, piloerection, and yawning are more typical for heroin withdrawal than for other CNS depressants.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is withdrawing from hydromorphone, an opiate. Opiates are central nervous system (CNS) depressants. Opiate intoxication causes sedation, bradycardia and hypotension, depressed respiratory drive, and constricted pupils. Opiates are commonly used for analgesia and act on receptors within the gastrointestinal tract, reducing gut motility and thus causing constipation. Opiate withdrawal presents with restlessness, tachycardia, hypertension, pupil dilation, tremors, muscle cramping, abdominal cramping, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Lacrimation and rhinorrhea can occur. Opiate withdrawal also features yawning and piloerection, which are not seen in withdrawal from other recreational drugs. Withdrawal from opiates is not fatal though is generally unpleasant for patients. Treatment is supportive and includes benzodiazepines, antiemetics, and clonidine during the acute withdrawal period, ideally with transition to a partial opioid agonist (eg, buprenorphine) or long-acting agonist (eg, methadone) if the patient is at risk for relapse. bradycardia and hypotension, depressed respiratory drive, and constricted pupils. questions.retrievalTag: Alcohol is a known teratogen that causes fetal alcohol syndrome
questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of hyperactivity and learning difficulties since starting kindergarten. She was born at term by an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery but had a period of increased irritability and tremulousness soon after birth. She is at the 20th percentile for height and the 30th percentile for weight. Examination shows short palpebral fissures, an upturned nose, a hypoplastic philtrum, and a cardiac murmur. She has an IQ of 60. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Exposure to Treponema pallidum in utero

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Fragile site on the X chromosome

questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol is a known teratogen that causes fetal alcohol syndrome when used during pregnancy. Fetal alcohol syndrome presents with intellectual disability and developmental delays, along with physical malformations such as microcephaly, facial dysmorphia, limb deformities, and congenital heart disease.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol is a proven teratogen, as alcohol use during pregnancy leads to irreversible fetal neurologic effects and physical malformations in a cluster of symptoms called fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS). FAS is the leading cause of intellectual disability in the United States and would explain this patient's learning difficulties and behavioral problems. As a result of the failure of cell migration, FAS also presents with physical abnormalities such as microcephaly, facial abnormalities (small palpebral fissures, thin vermilion border, cleft lip/palate, smooth philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. Patients with FAS may demonstrate abnormal bone development (eg, cervical spine fusion) and delayed bone growth. Though alcohol is associated with obstetric complications such as preterm birth that commonly result from the use of substances during pregnancy, the physical malformations, including those seen in this case, are specific to alcohol use. COOTER AE By, One r, Exposure to cocaine in utero (Choice B) causes vasoconstriction of the placental vasculature, which can lead to preterm birth, intrauterine growth restriction, low birth weight, and placental abruption. Maternal hypertension and preeclampsia can also result from the use of cocaine. Additionally, maternal cocaine use can cause vascular occlusion in a segment of bowel, resulting in jejunal or ileal atresia, Exposure to lead (Choice C) leads to toxic effects in children as a result of an immature central nervous system and lead passage across the blood-brain barrier. Clinical manifestations of lead poisoning include encephalopathy, cognitive dysfunction, peripheral nephropathy, hearing loss, renal tubular dysfunction, interstitial nephritis, vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation, and anemia. Management depends on the blood lead concentration and presence of symptoms, and includes chelation with dimercaprol, ethylene diamine tetraacetate, or succimer. Exposure to Treponema pallidum in utero (Choice D), specifically during the second or third trimester, causes congenital syphilis. Manifestations include syphilitic 'hinitis, rhagades around the mouth, dental malformations (including notched teeth and irregularly shaped molars), deafness, and organomegaly, but the time of presentation after birth is variable. Fragile site on the X chromosome (Choice E) is found in fragile X syndrome, an X-linked cause of inherited intellectual disability that is caused by a CGG- trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR? gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face with a prominent jaw, a high- arched palate, large ears, hyperextensible joints, and macroorchidism. This patient's birth history and constellation of physical features suggests fetal alcohol syndrome instead, Trisomy of a chromosome (Choice F) is observed in Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18), and Patau syndrome (trisomy 13). Edwards syndrome presents with characteristic physical features including intellectual disability, a prominent occiput, low-set ears, micrognathia, clenched, overlapping fingers, and feet with a prominent calcaneus and convexly rounded soles. Patau syndrome presents with characteristic physical features including intellectual disabilty, cleft lip and palate, holoprosencephaly, microphthalmia, cutis aplasia, feet with a prominent calcaneus and convexly rounded soles, and polydactyly.

His wife says that he has begun speaking oddly at times and mixing his words. He says that he feels fine, has a good appetite, and sleeps well. On mental status examination, he is oriented to time but not to person or place. He is able to recall zero of three objects after 5 minutes. Laboratory findings are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Dementia, Alzheimer type, is characterizing by progressive cognitive decline that culminates in the inability to perform basic activities of daily living. It is thought to be caused by decreased cholinergic signaling. Cholinesterase inhibitors such as donepezil are the first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Dementia, Alzheimer type, is the most prevalent dementia in elderly patients and is characterized by the loss of intellectual function within multiple domains, such as language (eg, word finding), memory, perception, motor skills, attention, orientation, problem-solving (eg, difficulty completing tasks), and executive function. It classically presents with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment, progresses to apraxia and language abnormalities, and culminates in behavioral and personality changes preventing the patient from performing basic activities of daily living. Alzheimer disease is postulated to be caused by decreased cholinergic signaling in the cortex and basal forebrain. Therefore, treatment consists of medications that increase cholinergic activity. questions.retrievalTag: Brain cancer is associated with major depressive disorder
questions.stem: A 39-year-old woman is examined 1 day after admission because she was found in an unresponsive state with an empty bottle of topiramate nearby. She overdosed with suicidal intent because she has felt "lost" since completing radiation therapy for a left frontoparietal oligodendroglioma 3 months ago. She has been ruminating about past mistakes. During this time, she has had decreased appetite resulting in a 7-kg (15-lb) weight loss. Her father and brother have bipolar disorder. BMI is 18 kg/m2. On mental status examination, she exhibits psychomotor slowing and has a depressed mood. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Brain cancer is associated with major depressive disorder. In patients with the combination of a low BMI and insomnia, mirtazapine is a reasonable first-line option given its adverse effect profile that includes weight gain and sedation.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Major depressive disorder (MDD) is the most likely diagnosis. Brain cancer is associated with depression, as frontal tumors may directly damage brain areas involved in regulating mood; brain cancer is a significant stressor, and radiation treatment can lead to depression (possibly via disrupting neurohormone signaling). 'Symptoms of MDD typically include two or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor slowing, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Many depressed patients engage in anxious rumination, MDD that includes anxiety symptoms can be termed "MDD with anxious features" in the DSM-5. This patient who recently attempted suicide and appears severely depressed should be admitted to the inpatient psychiatry unit, where the combination of antidepressant therapy and psychotherapy should be initiated. Initial antidepressant medication for most patients is selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor therapy. However, in patients with the combination of a low BMI and insomnia, mirtazapine is another reasonable firstline option given its adverse effect profile that includes weight gain and sedation. Mirtazapine, an atypical antidepressant medication, is a selective ay-receptor antagonist and serotonin modulator.

questions.retrievalTag: Fragile X syndrome is an X-linked cause of inherited intellectual
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of increasing problems since entering second grade. Although he repeated first grade, he continues to have difficulty reading and writing. His teacher says that he is immature and often teased by the other children. He is at the 10th percentile for height and 75th percentile for weight. He has a large head with a prominent forehead and jaw and large protruding ears. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's learning problems?
questions.educationalObjective: Fragile X syndrome is an X-linked cause of inherited intellectual disability that is caused by a CGG-trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. It presents with features including intellectual disability, elongated face, prominent jaw, large ears, and macroorchidism.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient's constellation of findings, including intellectual disability, an elongated face, prominent forehead, large jaw, and large ears, is suggestive of fragile X syndrome. Patients may also present with macroorchidism, hyperextensible joints, and a high-arched palate. Fragile X syndrome is the second most common cause of inherited intellectual disability after Down syndrome and is inherited in an X-linked fashion. Itis caused by a CGG-trinucleotide repeat expansion within the FMR? gene on the X chromosome. On karyotype, the end of the X chromosome appears truncated, leading to the name fragile X syndrome. Patients with fragile X syndrome are at increased risk for mitral valve prolapse and educational difficulties. They are often diagnosed with autism. As with all trinucleotide repeat expansions, genetic anticipation is seen, wherein future generations have increased severity and/or earlier onset of disease.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'expansions, genetic anticipation is seen, wherein future generations have increased severity and/or earlier onset of disease. - Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E. 'Chemical imbalance (Choice A) is postulated to be a cause of attention-deficithyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Children with predominant hyperactivity symptoms are tunable to sit still and may have difficulty taking turns (postulated to result from a dysregulated dopamine reward pathway), whereas children with predominant inattentive symptoms tend to daydream, process information slowly, and demonstrate difficulty in completing tasks (related to norepinephrine dysregulation). 'Chemical imbalance is also thought to cause depression and anxiety via dysregulation of serotonin pathways. This patient's unique physical characteristics are not features of any of these disorders. Importantly, chemical imbalance is a vague and colloquial term, and is not language commonly involved in the diagnosis of patients. Environmental toxin (Choice C) exposure should be suspected in children who present with acute- to subacute-onset symptoms of toxicity including encephalopathy, nausea, and vomiting. Further details of clinical presentation can suggest the likely causative agent. Commonly encountered toxins include lead (cognitive dysfunction, peripheral nephropathy, hearing loss, renal tubular dysfunction, interstitial nephritis, vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation, and anemia), ethylene <alycol (acute kidney injury, oxalate crystals on urinalysis, anion-gap metabolic acidosis), and jimson weed (tachycardia, mydriasis, urinary retention, xerostomia). Enzyme deficiency (Choice D), most often caused by a gene mutation, results in a wide variety of inborn errors of metabolism, many of which present with intellectual disablity. This patient's learning and behavioral difficulties, in combination with the characteristic physical features of a prominent forehead, large jaw, and large ears, are more consistent with fragile X syndrome. Exposure to the intrauterine teratogen (Choice E) alcohol is the leading cause of intellectual disability in the United States. As a result ofthe failure of cell migration, fetal alcohol syndrome also presents with physical abnormalities such as microcephaly, facial abnormalities (small palpebral fissures, thin vermilion border, cleft lipipalate, smooth philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. This patient's physical characteristics are more consistent with fragile X syndrome.

questions.retrievalTag: Carbidopa-levodopa non-selectively increases dopamine concentrations leading to improvement of Parkinson
questions.stem: A 77-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-week history of insomnia, agitation, and auditory hallucinations. He says he often hears accusatory voices. He has Parkinson disease that has been treated with carbidopa-levodopa therapy for the past 3 years. Physical examination shows a rhythmic resting tremor. On mental status examination, he appears agitated. He reports continued auditory hallucinations. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Add citalopram to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Add haloperidol to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Add lorazepam to the medication regimen

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Add olanzapine to the medication regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Carbidopa-levodopa non-selectively increases dopamine concentrations, leading to improvement of Parkinson symptoms but increasing the risk for psychotic symptoms. Psychotic symptoms in patients with Parkinson disease should be treated with a dose decrease of carbidopa-levodopa, quetiapine, or clozapine.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by degradation of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Symptoms are associated with an imbalance of dopamine and acetylcholine, resulting in the characteristic findings of bradykinesia, resting tremor, muscle rigidity, and postural instability Psychiatric disturbances, sleep disorders, and dementia are common complications in addition to prominent motor symptoms. This patient with new-onset psychosis 'may be experiencing Parkinson disease-related psychosis, a carbidopa-levodopa adverse effect, or both. Carbidopa-levodopa increases dopamine concentrations in several brain areas. In the basal ganglia, an increased dopamine concentration leads to improvement in Parkinson symptoms. In the limbic system, an increased dopamine concentration leads to psychotic symptoms such as paranoid delusions or hallucinations. Distressing psychotic symptoms in the setting of Parkinson may be experiencing Parkinson disease-related psychosis, a carbidopa-levodopa adverse effect, or both. Carbidopa-levodopa increases dopamine concentrations in several brain areas. In the basal ganglia, an increased dopamine concentration leads to improvement in Parkinson symptoms. In the limbic system, an increased dopamine concentration leads to psychotic symptoms such as paranoid delusions or hallucinations. Distressing psychotic symptoms in the setting of Parkinson disease are typically managed with a decreased dose of carbidopa-levodopa or antipsychotic medication. This patient demonstrates mild Parkinson symptoms currently, so the physician should first attempt decreasing the carbidopa-levodopa dose. If this strategy is ineffective in addressing psychotic symptoms or significantly worsens Parkinson symptoms, then the physician should increase carbidopa-levodopa to its previous dose and instead add an antipsychotic medication. 'Atypical antipsychotics, especially quetiapine and clozapine, less potently antagonize dopamine receptors and modulate other neurotransmitters such as serotonin (which assist in regulating dopamine signaling in the limbic system and thus indirectly address psychotic symptoms). Consequently, quetiapine and clozapine improve psychotic symptoms without significantly exacerbating Parkinson symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Adding citalopram (Choice A) and lorazepam (Choice C) could help with this patient's anxiety. However, neither medication antagonizes dopamine receptors, so his psychotic symptoms would be unlikely to improve, 'Adding haloperidol (Choice B), a typical antipsychotic that potently antagonizes dopamine receptors in both the basal ganglia and limbic system, would improve psychotic symptoms but would potentially exacerbate the patient's Parkinson symptoms. Typical antipsychotics should be avoided in patients with Parkinson disease, 'Adding olanzapine (Choice D), an atypical antipsychotic that antagonizes dopamine receptors more potently than quetiapine or clozapine, Is likely to exacerbate Parkinson symptoms. Quetiapine and clozapine are the best options for treating psychosis in Parkinson disease. Increasing the dose of carbidopa-levodopa (Choice F) is likely to exacerbate this patient's psychotic symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Premature ejaculation a type of sexual dysfunction is commonly associated
questions.stem: A 29-year-old man with asthma comes to the clinic. He has a 7-month history of sexual difficulties. He has had premature ejaculation during sexual activity, with ejaculation occurring within approximately 30 seconds of vaginal penetration. The patient says that he has begun to avoid sexual activity because of embarrassment. Vital signs and physical examination are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?
questions.educationalObjective: Premature ejaculation, a type of sexual dysfunction, is commonly associated with psychological factors such as performance anxiety. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, which can delay ejaculation, are the first-line pharmacological treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient demonstrates premature ejaculation, a type of sexual dysfunction. Psychological factors such as performance anxiety or marital conflict can exert a strong influence on sexual function. In males, anxiety can lead to decreased libido, decreased ability to obtain or maintain an erection, premature ejaculation, or decreased ability to ejaculate. Patients with sexual dysfunction secondary to performance anxiety typically continue to experience nocturnal tumescence and 'emissions and lack anatomical abnormalities of the genitalia on physical examination. Treatment strategies for sexual dysfunction include cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness meditation training, or pharmacotherapy. First-line pharmacotherapy for premature ejaculation is selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) therapy, particularly paroxetine. SSRIs have an unclear mechanism for delaying ejaculation that likely involves descending serotonin pathways.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Buspirone (Choice A) is a serotonin modulator useful in generalized anxiety disorder. It has no use in premature ejaculation. Sildenafil (Choice C) is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor that promotes vasodilation in the arteries of the corpora cavernosa. Sildenafil can be helpful in delaying ejaculation in patients with both premature ejaculation and erectile dysfunction, but SSRIs have more evidence for treating premature ejaculation alone. Testosterone (Choice D) is utilized to treat sexual dysfunction in patients with hypogonadism. Hypogonadism is more associated with low libido than premature ejaculation, and this patient lacks other features of hypogonadism such as decreased body hair, muscle mass, and energy. Trazodone (Choice E) is a serotonin modulator historically utilized as an antidepressant that is now more commonly used for insomnia. It is associated with priapism in male patients and has not been shown to help with premature ejaculation.

questions.retrievalTag: Panic disorder is an anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent panic
questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of palpitations, hyperventilation, dizziness, and light-headedness. Her husband says she has had similar episodes weekly during the past year. He says his wife is reluctant to leave the house without him because she is fearful of having an episode in public. She prefers to shop for groceries late at night when few people are around. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Panic disorder is an anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. The avoidance of panic attack triggers may lead to agoraphobia.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and include four or more additional physical symptoms of sympathetic hyperarousal or associated mental states. These additional symptoms may include heart palpitations or tachycardia, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, the sensation of choking, gastrointestinal distress, paresthesia, sweating, chills, trembling, derealization, the fear of dying, and/or the fear of losing control. Panic disorder is an anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. The pathogenesis involves genetic factors, an anxious temperament, and stressful life events leading to catastrophic appraisals of internal body states such as normal sympathetic activity. The avoidance of panic attack triggers may lead to agoraphobia, as in this patient. In patients with an initial episode of panic 'symptoms, a thorough history and physical examination along with a basic laboratory workup and a potential ECG should be performed to exclude medical causes of sympathetic hyperarousal, Treatment of panic disorder typically includes antidepressant medication and/or cognitive behavioral therapy with a focus on decreasing patients' catastrophic appraisal of bodily sensations of sympathetic hyperarousal and increasing patients' abilty to cope with panic attack sensations.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Meniere disease (Choice A) is a condition of episodic vertigo and sensorineural hearing loss that results from pressure accumulation in the endolymph of the inner ear. Patients typically present with episodic vertigo, hearing loss, and tinnitus. This patient does not experience hearing loss and experiences symptoms of 'sympathetic activation (eg, palpitations, hyperventilation) that are uncommon in Meniere disease. Mitral valve prolapse (Choice B) is often asymptomatic, but if presenting with symptoms, may be accompanied by chest discomfort, dyspnea on exertion, exercise intolerance, and palpitations. Though panic attacks may develop over time because of these symptoms, this patient demonstrates preoccupation with her 'sympathetic activation symptoms and agoraphobia, which are more consistent with panic disorder. Cardiac auscultation typically reveals a mid-systolic click followed by alate systolic murmur, not a systolic ejection murmur. Paroxysmal atrial tachycardia (Choice D) is a type of supraventricular tachycardia originating from structural cardiac abnormalities or conduction abnormalities that can present with dizziness, dyspnea, palpitations, and/or chest pain. A narrow QRS complex is typically seen on ECG. This patient demonstrates preoccupation with decreasing patients' catastrophic appraisal of bodily sensations of sympathetic hyperarousal and increasing patients' ability to cope with panic attack sensations. questions.retrievalTag: Delirium is an acute confusional state resulting from medical illness
questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidemia is brought to the clinic because of a 4-day history of behavioral changes. She has recently been awake much of the night, paranoid that people are coming into the house and poisoning her food. Her mother appeared to be talking to herself. The patient is calm and pleasant, and says her daughter is overreacting. She is oriented to person and place but not to time. Mental status examination discloses impaired attention and concentration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium is an acute confusional state resulting from medical illness, substance use, and/or medications. It presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition that fluctuate in severity.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Delirium is an acute confusional state typically resulting from acute medical illness, sleep disturbances, substance use, and/or medications in older patients who possess a decreased cognitive reserve (eg, patients with dementia), such as this patient with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia who likely has chronic microvascular brain changes. Delirium presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition that fluctuate in severity over the course of the day. This patient's fluctuating course is reflected in her pleasant and nonchalant demeanor in the examination room compared to her paranoid ideation at night. questions.retrievalTag: Avoidant personality disorder features the avoidance of interpersonal contact because
questions.stem: A 39-year-old man comes to the office for follow-up of a 10-year history of major depressive disorder. He recently changed jobs from a night shift to a day shift. He describes himself as shy, overly cautious, and generally pessimistic. He says he is unable to assert himself. He believes he is "empathic, hard-working, and loyal." He has few friends and has difficulty making new ones. He craves affection, but he has difficulty establishing close relationships because of his shyness. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
questions.educationalObjective: Avoidant personality disorder features the avoidance of interpersonal contact because of feelings of social inadequacy and hypersensitivity to rejection. Psychotherapy, particularly cognitive behavioral therapy, is the cornerstone of treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Avoidant personality disorder is a cluster C personality disorder, the anxious or fearful cluster, that features the persistent avoidance of interpersonal contact (eg, taking night shifts to avoid human contact) because of low self-esteem and hypersensitivity to rejection. Consequently, these patients are frequently non-assertive and suppress emotional expression. Avoidant personality disorder is closely related to social anxiety disorder. Some conceptualize it as a more severe subset of social anxiety disorder. Others view social anxiety disorder as inability to perform in social situations as opposed to patients' inabilty to relate socially in avoidant personality disorder. A combination of genetic factors, temperamental factors (eg, rigidity, behavioral inhibition, hypersensitivity), and a dismissive or guilting parent likely promotes the development of avoidant personality disorder. Psychotherapy rather than pharmacotherapy is the cornerstone of personality disorder treatment. 'Though few studies exist, helpful therapy modalities for avoidant personality disorder likely include cognitive behavioral therapy (with a focus on decreasing automatic negative thoughts about the self and exposure to social situations), psychodynamic psychotherapy, and supportive psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Lorazepam (Choice B) can be utilized as a treatment for refractory social anxiety disorder, and Quetiapine (Choice D) has a small amount of evidence for social anxiety disorder. Pharmacotherapy is less helpful than psychotherapy in personality disorders. Motivational interviewing (Choice C) addresses ambivalence about health behavior change using open-ended, non-judgmental questions to help the patient explore their reasons for wanting to change or maintain a health habit (eg, diet, smoking, substance use). Avoidant behaviors constitute a personality trait, not a health habit. No management is indicated (Choice E) is incorrect, as physicians commonly address personality disorder symptoms through psychotherapy.

questions.retrievalTag: Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms
questions.stem: An otherwise healthy 42-year-old man comes to the office because his wife can no longer tolerate his moodiness. He says he has not enjoyed "daily activities most of the time" during the past decade. He says he frequently feels like he "won't be good enough" and has no hope that he will feel better. He is a computer programmer and performs well on the job. He had a successful treatment of depressed mood from ages 19-20. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least two years. Other symptoms include neurovegetative symptoms, impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Persistent depressive disorder, previously called dysthymia, refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least two years. Patients must report a depressed mood on more days than not. Other symptoms of persistent depressive disorder include neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness. This patient reports a depressed mood and hopelessness. Though this patient's depressive symptoms influenced his psychological response to the return of his son, the symptoms preceded this stressor. Cyclothymic disorder (Choice B) presents with hypomanic and depressive symptoms, which fail to meet diagnostic criteria for a hypomanic or depressive episode. 'Symptoms must last for at least two years and must not cause significant impairment. This patient does not report a history of hypomanic symptoms. Dependent personality disorder (Choice C) is one of the cluster C personality disorders, the anxious or fearful cluster. Patients with dependent personality disorder persistently demonstrate an excessive need to be cared for by others that manifests as severe separation anxiety and clinging behavior. 'These symptoms significantly impair patients' daily function. This patient reports fewer than five depressive symptoms, and though his symptoms are affecting his relationship with his wife, he is not experiencing serious daily dysfunction. In addition, major depressive episodes are typically shorter-lived than this patient's persistent depressive symptoms.

She has been increasingly confused and forgetful. Physical examination shows dry mucous membranes, nystagmus, and somnolence. Gait is mildly ataxic. The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is a deficiency of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Hyperemesis gravidarum rarely leads to vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency and consequent Wernicke encephalopathy. The triad is acutely altered mental status, ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia.

body: Folic acid (Choice A) deficiency typically presents with fatigue, diarthea, anemia, and anorexia. Patients may demonstrate a beefy red tongue and pallor on physical examination. Confusion is more characteristic of vitamin B (thiamine) deficiency. Vitamin D (Choice €) deficiency presents in adults with diffuse myalgias, muscle weakness, bone pain, and fragility fractures. questions.retrievalTag: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity and/or inattention
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy has a 2-year history of behavioral problems and declining academic performance. His parents say he has always been very active and has had difficulty concentrating and completing tasks. In the examination room, he jumps on and off the examination table and frequently interrupts his mother. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Ask parents and teachers to complete a standardized questionnaire

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Reassure parents that behavior is normal

questions.educationalObjective: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity and/or inattention that occur in more than one setting. Diagnosis requires parents and teachers fill out behavioral rating scales.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: 'Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a heritable neurodevelopmental disorder that presents with chronic symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity and/or inattention that occur in more than one setting and impair academic, social, and/or emotional function. Children with predominant hyperactivity symptoms are unable to sit still and may have difficulty taking turns generally and in conversation (postulated to result from a dysregulated dopamine reward pathway), whereas children with predominant inattentive symptoms tend to daydream, process information slowly, and demonstrate difficulty in completing tasks (related to norepinephrine dysregulation), This patient demonstrates both inattentive and hyperactive symptoms. For diagnosis, physicians must establish that the patient is functionally impaired in several settings, so parents and teachers must fll out behavioral rating scales. Stimulants, which include amphetamine salts and methylphenidate, are first-line agents for treatment that increase synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'Advise the parents to remove caffeine from the patient's diet (Choice A) can help decrease patients' anxiety but is unlikely to meaningfully affect ADHD symptoms. If, this patient's hyperactivity symptoms were caused by caffeine intake, the symptoms would be episodic with caffeine withdrawal and slowed activity between episodes. Begin stimulant therapy (Choice C) would be premature. The physician first needs to establish the ADHD diagnosis. Order thyroid function tests (Choice D) would assist in diagnosing thyroid dysfunction, which may present with hyperactivity. However, this patient does not demonstrate tachycardia or physical examination signs of thyroid dysfunction (eg, skin, hair, or eye changes). Reassure the patient's parents that his behavior is normal for his age (Choice E) would be inappropriate, as this patient demonstrates impairing inattention and hyperactivity symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Toluene inhalant abuse can manifest with perioral dermatitis conjunctival injection
questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy is brought to the ED after his mother found him breaking the mirrors and windows in their home. He appeared confused. Examination shows mild erythema surrounding the mouth. There is a fruity odor to the breath. His movements are slow, and his gait is unsteady. His speech is slow. Intoxication with which of the following substances is the most likely cause?
questions.educationalObjective: Toluene inhalant abuse can manifest with perioral dermatitis, conjunctival injection, unusual breath odor, nausea, and neuropsychiatric symptoms such as irritability and mood swings.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Inhalant abuse (eg, glue, spray paint, shoe polish, toluene, nitrous oxide) is common in children and adolescents and has effects on multiple organ systems: neuropsychiatric (headache, cognitive impairment, anosmia, cerebellar dysfunction, mood swings, irritability, hallucinations), dermatologic (perioral or perinasal dermatitis), otolaryngologic (nosebleeds, halitosis), ocular (conjunctival injection), cardiac (dysrhythmia, tachycardia), gastrointestinal (nausea, anorexia), and respiratory (wheezing, coughing, sneezing). This patient's recent confusion, ataxia, and poor school functioning are likely related to the neuropsychiatric effects of inhalants. Toluene use especially is characterized by acute slurred speech, disorientation, hallucinations, agitation, and violent behavior. It also causes dermatitis and erythema in the perioral area due to the drying effects of hydrocarbons. Treatment is primarily supportive, though some inhalants have specific antidotes (eg, 'methylene blue for nitrites). Prevention is key; schools should monitor use of solvent-based products and parents and children should be educated about the risks of inhalants.

questions.retrievalTag: Huntington disease is associated with expansion of a trinucleotide repeat
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man has a gradual change in personality during the past 2 years. He has become more self-centered and inconsiderate. His mother committed suicide at approximately 40 years of age. On mental status examination, his mood is euphoric. On physical examination, saccades are slowed and there are occasional jerky movements of the face and trunk muscles. Which of the following is most likely to establish the diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Huntington disease is associated with expansion of a trinucleotide repeat (CAG) on chromosome four. It presents with psychiatric and cognitive symptoms, followed by motor symptoms. Diagnosis is confirmed with genetic testing.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Genetic testing will likely confirm a diagnosis of Huntington disease (HD), which is a hereditary, progressive neurodegenerative disorder affecting the caudate nuclei in the basal ganglia, typically arising in the third to sixth decades of life. The disease usually presents with an insidious onset of psychiatric (irritability, behavioral changes, depression, psychosis) and cognitive symptoms. Motor symptoms (chorea, hypotonia, dystonia, loss of voluntary control of movements) typically appear later in the disease course. People with HD typically die 10 to 40 years after disease onset; suicide is a common cause of death. Chorea, a key HD symptom, can be later in the disease course. People with HD typically die 10 to 40 years after disease onset; suicide is a common cause of death. Chorea, a key HD symptom, can be. initially experienced as restlessness. Chorea is distinguished by irregular, unpredictable muscle movements that tend to flow from one muscle group to another. Treatment is symptomatic and can include tetrabenazine or deutetrabenazine, which target chorea by depleting dopamine stores. HD is caused by expansion of a trinucleotide repeat (CAG) on chromosome four, which occurs when a series of three nucleotides in repetition are replicated incorrectly and too many repeats are included in the replicated product. HD also demonstrates anticipation, a genetic inheritance phenomenon in which future generations are affected at a younger age oF with more severe symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: 'No further testing (Choice A) is not appropriate at this time given the impact of the patient's symptoms and abnormalities noted on neurologic examination. Obtaining a diagnosis is important so that the appropriate management can be initiated. 'Complete psychiatric assessment (Choice B) may benefit the patient if the diagnostic workup for organic disease is inconclusive. Laboratory and genetic testing for neurologic disorders should be performed first given his abnormal neurologic examination. Neuropsychological testing (Choice C) may be useful to assess the patient's cognitive and motor function and track evolution over time. However, genetic testing to Confirm or rule out the suspected cause of the patient's presentation should be pursued first. MRI of the brain (Choice E) may reveal atrophy of the caudate nucleus, which correlates with progression of cognitive impairment in HD. However, diagnosis must be established first, and genetic testing is more sensitive and specific than neuroimaging in the evaluation of suspected HD. Electroencephalography (Choice F) is unlikely to provide useful information in this case. The patient's involuntary movements are more consistent with chorea than with epilepsy.

questions.retrievalTag: At toxic doses TCAs are associated with strong anticholinergic properties
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the ED 90 minutes after ingesting 25 100-mg amitriptyline tablets. She has a 10-year history of self-destructive behavior. Temperature is 38°C, pulse 108, BP 90/60. Pupils are 4 mm and reactive. She is intermittently loud and argumentative. Which of the following abnormalities is most likely to be noted on this patient's ECG?
questions.educationalObjective: At toxic doses, TCAs are associated with strong anticholinergic properties, seizures, and quinidine-like effects on cardiac conduction (eg, QRS-complex prolongation, AV block).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient with likely borderline personality disorder (as evidenced by her recurrent suicidal gestures, tendency toward anger, and unstable interpersonal telationships) has overdosed on amitriptyline, a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA). At therapeutic doses, TCAs demonstrate mild anticholinergic activity. At toxic doses, TCAs are associated with strong anticholinergic properties, seizures, and quinidine-lke effects on cardiac conduction (eg, QRS-complex prolongation, AV block). Anticholinergic toxicity typically manifests as sedation, delirium, hallucinations, ataxia, mydriasis, visual disturbances (cycloplegia, or loss of accommodation), dry 'mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation, and flushed skin. In patients demonstrating TCA toxicity, the offending medication should be discontinued and the QRS interval should be closely monitored. In patients with TCA toxicity and a QRS interval greater than 100 ms or a ventricular arrhythmia, sodium bicarbonate should be administered.

questions.retrievalTag: Obsessive-compulsive disorder manifests as recurrent intrusive urges or thoughts that
questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl has become increasingly focused on cleanliness. She now takes long showers, cleans her room constantly, and frequently washes her hands. She reports she is aware of germs and cannot stop worrying about them. Examination shows excoriated, irritated skin over the dorsum of the hands. What is the most appropriate management for her underlying disorder?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts that the patient tries to relieve by repetitive rituals. First-line therapy for pediatric OCD is cognitive behavioral therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive, anxiety-inducing urges or thoughts (obsessions) that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals (compulsions). Obsessions cause significant anxiety, and compulsions are frequently time-consuming and decrease patients' daily function. 'Common obsessions are related to cleanliness (as in this patient), symmetry, harm to self or others, and taboo thoughts or ideas. Compulsions are rigidly applied repetitive behaviors (eg, checking, washing) or mental activities (eg, counting, repeating words) that relieve anxiety related to obsessions. OCD typically presents relatively early in life, with most cases presenting by age 20 years. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is the cornerstone of treatment. CBT for OCD promotes the habituation of anxiety by having the patient experience their obsessions without doing the time-consuming compulsions. Psychotherapy is the preferred treatment for children with OCD, though selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are second-line treatments.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Buspirone (Choice A) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. It has no use in OCD. Family therapy (Choice C) is helpful for resolving interpersonal conflicts within families. Though families are encouraged to participate in CBT for OCD, family therapy is not beneficial. Risperidone therapy (Choice D) may be utilized to augment SSRI therapy in refractory OCD. However, CBT is the first-line treatment. Social skils training (Choice €) is a management strategy for schizophrenia. It has no use in OCD. Venlafaxine therapy (Choice F) is sometimes used as a second-line treatment in adults with OCD. It is not a preferred medication for pediatric OCD.
